IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
I.A.NO.83247 OF 2026
IN
CIVIL APPEAL NOS.7273-7275 OF 2013
 
B.V. HARINARAYAN REDDY        …APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
KUMARI CHUKKI NUNJUNDA 
SWAMY  & ORS.   …RESPONDENT(S)
 
O R D E R
1. The  present  application  has  been  filed  by  the
applicant-appellant praying for the following reliefs:
“a)  take  on  record  the  Memorandum  of
Understanding  dated  09.03.2026  executed
between the Appellant and Respondent Nos. 1,
2, 4 and 5 and annexed hereto as ANNEXURE A-
1;
b) dispose of the present Civil Appeal as settled
in terms of the Memorandum of Understanding
dated 09.03.2026, and record that no dispute
survives between the Appellant and Respondent
Nos.  1,  2,  4  and  5  in  respect  of  the  subject
matter of the present Appeal;
c) direct the refund of the sum of Rs.17,80,000/-
(Rupees Seventeen Lakh Eighty Thousand only)
along with interest @ 9% per annum from the
date of deposit till the date of actual payment,
C.A.NOS.7273-7275 OF 2013 1

--- Page 349 ---
deposited before the Ld. II Additional Principal
Judge, Family Court at Bangalore in FDP No.
1/2002;
d)  pass  such  further  orders  as  this  Hon’ble
Court may deem fit and proper.”
2. The  civil  appeals  arise  out  of  the  judgment  and
order dated 12.09.2011 passed by the High Court of
Karnataka at Bangalore in Writ Petition Nos. 7578-
7580 of 2010. The dispute pertains to proceedings
arising from (final decree proceedings) FDP No. 1 of
2002, in which the appellant had purchased the
subject  property  in  a  court  auction  and  had
deposited  a  sum  of  Rs.17,80,000/-  before  the
learned  Trial  Court.  The  matter  has  remained
pending before this Court for a considerable length
of time. 
3. During the pendency of the present appeals, the
appellant and Respondent Nos. 1, 2, 4 and 5 have
amicably resolved their disputes. Pursuant to the
settlement,  the  subject  property  has  been  jointly
sold  to  a  third-party  purchaser,  namely,  Mr.  T.
Dinesh Karle, under registered Sale Deed No. JPN-
1-07900-2025-26  dated  09.03.2026  for  a  total
consideration of Rs.7,60,00,000/-. The settlement
records  that  the  sale  consideration  has  been
received and apportioned between the parties. The
C.A.NOS.7273-7275 OF 2013 2

--- Page 350 ---
Memorandum of Understanding dated 09.03.2026
further records that no dispute survives between
the parties in respect of the subject matter of the
present appeals. 
4. It is also stated that Respondent No. 3 is not a
necessary  party  to  the  settlement,  as  he  had
already  transferred  his  interest  in  favour  of
Respondent Nos. 1 and 2, who are parties to the
Memorandum of Understanding. In view of the said
clarification  placed  on  record,  the  non-signing  of
the Memorandum of Understanding by Respondent
No. 3 does not come in the way of recording the
settlement between the contesting parties. 
5. We are happy  to  note  that  a dispute which  has
remained pending for a long period has now been
brought  to  an  end  by  mutual  settlement.  Such
resolution  gives  quietus  to  the  lis between  the
parties  and  also  prevents  further  multiplicity  of
proceedings in respect of the subject property. 
6. From the above reliefs, Mr. Nakul Dewan, learned
Senior  Counsel  appearing  for  the  applicant-
appellant, submits that in Clause C the demand
interest at the rate of 9% per on the deposits lying
with the Second Additional Principal Judge, Family
Court at Bangalore, may be deleted and may be
C.A.NOS.7273-7275 OF 2013 3

--- Page 351 ---
substituted by any interest on the deposit of Rs.
17,80,000/-.
7. Accordingly,  the  II  Additional  Principal  Judge,
Family Court, Bangalore, before whom FDP No. 1 of
2002 was pending, is directed to release/refund the
amount  of  Rs.17,80,000/-  deposited  by  the
appellant, together with such interest as may have
accrued thereon, if any, in accordance with law and
after due verification. 
8. The present civil appeals are taken on board. The
Memorandum of Understanding dated 09.03.2026
is taken on record. 
9. The  civil  appeals  shall  stand  disposed  of  in  the
same terms. 
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of. 
 
……………………………………J.
(VIKRAM NATH)
……………………………………J. 
 (SANDEEP MEHTA)
NEW DELHI
MAY 15, 2026

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the 
following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner was put to trial in the Court of the
Additional  Sessions  Judge  VII,  East  Singhbhum,
SLP(Crl.)  No.7243/2026                                     2

--- Page 355 ---
Jamshedpur  in  connection  with  S.T.No.486  of  2017
arising out of Sonari Police Station Case No. 66 of
2017 for the offence punishable under Sections 302 and
304-B of the Indian Penal Code, 1860, respectively (for
short, “the IPC”).
2. At the end of the trial, the petitioner came to be
acquitted by the Trial Court insofar as the offence
under  Section  302  and  Section  304B  of  the  IPC,
respectively, is concerned.
3. However, on the very same set of evidence, the
Trial  Court  held  the  petitioner  guilty  of  having
committed offence of abetment of suicide punishable
under Section 306 of the IPC.
4. The  deceased  happened  to  be  the  wife  of  the
petitioner.
5. It appears to be the case of the State that the
case is one of murder for want of dowry.
SLP(Crl.)  No.7243/2026                                     3

--- Page 356 ---
6. However, the State was unable to establish its case
of murder for want of dowry.  The State has not come in
appeal against that part of the order passed by the
Trial Court acquitting the petitioner.
7. Ultimately,  the  Trial  Court  sentenced  the
petitioner to undergo 06 years of rigorous imprisonment
with fine of Rs.10,000/- (Rupees Ten thousand only).
8. We heard Mr. Himanshu Shekhar, the learned counsel
appearing for the petitioner, Mr. Rakesh Kumar Singh,
the  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  de  facto
complainant; and Ms.Pallavi Langar, the learned counsel
appearing for the State.
9. Having regard to the nature of the evidence on
record and also having regard to the fact that the
petitioner has been sentenced to undergo fixed term of
06 years, we are persuaded to exercise our discretion
in favour of the petitioner.
SLP(Crl.)  No.7243/2026                                     4

--- Page 357 ---
10. We order that the substantive order of sentence of
fixed term shall remain suspended and the petitioner be
released on bail  forthwith, if not required in any
other case, subject to terms and conditions that the
Trial Court may deem fit to impose pending the final
disposal of Criminal Appeal No. 939 of 2025 pending
before the High Court.
11. With the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petition
stands disposed of.
12. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.
(CHANDRESH)                              (POOJA SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS               COURT MASTER (NSH)

O R D E R
1. The petitioner Nos. 185 and 274 (Jasbir Singh
and Krishan Kumar Verma) in Writ Petition (C)
No.557 of 2025 are present and duly represented by
their counsel. They have made a statement that
they do not wish to prosecute this petition any
longer as, apparently, they have been granted the
relief which they are seeking.
2. Accordingly, I.A. Nos. 101049 and 101053 of
2026 are allowed and the names of the petitioners,
namely,  Jasbir  Singh  (Petitioner  No.185)  and
Krishan Kumar Verma (Petitioner No.274) be scored
off from the array of parties in Writ Petition (C)
No.557 of 2025.
3. It has been pointed out by the learned counsel for
the petitioner(s) that the relief claimed in these
1

--- Page 364 ---
petition(s) stand squarely covered by an order of this
Court  dated  09.09.2024  passed  in  Special  Leave
Petition (Civil) No.2944 of 2024, titled  ‘Union of
India & Ors. vs. Prakash Bhaskar Chaudhari Deceased
Through LR. & Ors.’  and connected cases .  The said
order has been filed along with the counter affidavit
of the respondents as Annexure R-4. The said order
dated 09.09.2024 is reproduced hereunder:
“1. Delay condoned. Abatement, if any, is set
aside.
2. Applications for substitution are allowed.
3. We have perused the impugned orders of the
learned Central Administrative Tribunal as well
as various High Courts.
4. We agree with the views taken by the learned
Central  Administrative  Tribunal  that  the
respondents/employees were discriminated in the
matter of grant of benefit of Fifth Central Pay
Commission and the High Court has rightly held
that in order to bring them on parity with the
other similarly circumstances employees, they
were entitled to the revised pay scale with
effect from 01.01.1996.
5. We direct the petitioner(s) to clear the
arrears within a period of six months from
today.
6. The special leave petitions are, accordingly,
disposed of.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.”
4. Mr. Anil Kaushik, learned Additional Solicitor
General,  appearing  for  the  respondents,  does  not
W.P.(C) NO.557 OF 2025 & connected case
2

--- Page 365 ---
dispute the submission advanced by the learned counsel
for the petitioner(s).
5. Accordingly, the present petition(s) are disposed
of in the same terms.
6. The  petitioner(s)  would  be  entitled  to  the
relief(s)  as  have  been  granted  by  order  dated
09.09.2024.
7. The respondents would comply with the same within
the same time as allowed by the aforesaid orders.
8. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.
....................,J.
       (VIKRAM NATH)
....................,J.
   (SANDEEP MEHTA)
  NEW DELHI;  
MAY 19, 2026.

--- Page 368 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO(s).       OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.9204/2026)
THE CEOA EDUCATIONAL SOCIETY 
& ANR.                             APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
M. THILAGA TERENCY & ORS.         RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard Mr. S Nagamuthu, learned senior
counsel appearing for the appellants and Mr. P.
Soma  Sundaram,  learned  Advocate-on-Record
appearing on caveat for the respondent No.1.
3. The order impugned dated 07.05.2026 is an
order passed by the High Court in Writ Petition
Criminal(MD)  No.  2730/2026  wherein  the
challenge was to the summoning order dated
29.04.2026. 
4. The High Court while entertaining the
aforesaid  petition  has  passed  interim

--- Page 369 ---
2
directions,  one  in  respect  of  the  house
situated in Survey No.182/1B and the other in
respect of another building situated in the
same survey number. The High Court in respect
of  the  another  building  has  injuncted  the
appellants from interfering with the peaceful
possession of the appellants’ property situated
in Survey No. 182/1B.
5. The  submission  of  Mr.  S  Nagamuthu,
learned senior counsel for the appellants is
that in a writ petition wherein summoning order
is under challenge on the criminal side, the
High Court had no jurisdiction whatsoever to
grant injunction of the nature as passed under
the impugned order.
6. We are surprised to note the manner in
which the injunction has been granted that too
in a writ jurisdiction on a criminal side where
the challenge is only to the summoning order
dated 29.04.2026.
7. In  view  of  the  aforesaid  facts  and
circumstances, the order impugned insofar as it
injuncts the appellants cannot be sustained in

--- Page 370 ---
3
law. Accordingly, we set aside the part of the
impugned  order  insofar  as  it  injuncts  the
appellants from interfering with the possession
of  the  appellants’  property  in  Survey  No.
182/1B.
8. The appeal is allowed, accordingly, with
liberty to the respondents to take recourse to
the other remedies that may be available to
them  in  law  to  protect  their
interest/possession  over  their  property
situated in Survey No. 182/1B.
9. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall
stand disposed of.
…………………………………………………...J.
           [PANKAJ MITHAL]
…………………………………………………...J.
           [S.V.N. BHATTI]
NEW DELHI;
MAY 21, 2026.
SD

--- Page 371 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.           OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.6006 of 2026)
 PISHOR SINGH                                 APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
 STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR.                       RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
17.02.2026 in CRA-S No.2335 of 2025, titled “Pishor Singh
Vs. State of Punjab and Anr. ”, passed by the High Court
of Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh.
3. On  21.04.2026,  this  Court  passed  the  following
order:-
“1.  The  petitioner  challenges  the
judgment and order dated 17.02.2026 in
CRAS No. 2335/2025 passed by the High
Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh.
2.  Learned  counsel  for  the  petitioner
submits that, prior to the registration
of  the  instant  FIR  dated  05.07.2025,
which  was  as  a  consequence  of  the
complaint dated 08.04.2024 filed by the
private  respondent,  the  instant
petitioner  had  instituted  proceedings
against private respondent no.2 under the
provisions  of  Section  138  of  the
Negotiable  Instruments  Act,  1881  on
11.03.2024. Though the proceedings in the
said  complaint  are  pending,  however,
before the Panchayat, the petitioner and
1

--- Page 374 ---
private respondent no.2 entered into a
settlement  dated  18.03.2024.  It  is
further submitted that the instant FIR
dated 05.07.2025 is merely a counterblast
to  the  proceedings  initiated  by  the
petitioner,  and  that  the  alleged
allegations pertain to the period prior
to 18.03.2024.
3.  Issue  notice,  returnable  on
22.05.2026.
4.  Mr.  Siddhant  Sharma,  Advocate-on-
Record,  accepts  notice  on  behalf  of
respondent No.1-State of Punjab. Hence,
formal service of notice on respondent
No.1  -State  is  waived.  Let  notice  be
issued to respondent No.2.
5.  Dasti  service,  in  addition,  is
permitted. Let steps for service be taken
within one week.
6.  In  the  notice  itself,  let  it  be
mentioned that respondent is required to
file a counter affidavit and reply to the
interlocutory  application(s),  if  any,
before the next date of listing.
7. Learned counsel for the petitioner to
serve a copy of the present petition to
the learned counsel for the respondent
No.1-State within one week.
8. Let counter affidavit be filed within
two weeks. Rejoinder thereto, if any, be
filed within one week thereafter.
9. In the event of arrest in connection
with  FIR  No.  165  dated  05.07.2025,
registered under Section 376 of the IPC
and  Section  3(1)(w)  of  the  Scheduled
Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention
of Atrocities) Act, 1989 (as amended in
2015), at Police Station Sadar Faridkot,
District Faridkot, the petitioner shall
2

--- Page 375 ---
be  released  on  bail  by  the
Investigating/Arresting  Officer  on  such
terms and conditions as imposed and found
to be just, fair and reasonable.
10.  The  petitioner  shall  make  himself
available  before  the  Investigating
Officer on 27.04.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and
on all such dates as he may be required.
11. Needless to add, the petitioner would
maintain good conduct and not attempt to
influence any of the witnesses in any
manner till the completion of the trial.”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order
dated 21.04.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary
adjournment. Also, the appellant shall report before the
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10
a.m. and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is
complete in all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial
3

--- Page 376 ---
Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on
account of the appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail.
9. Further, if the appellant possesses a passport, the
same  shall  be  surrendered  before  the  Competent
Authority/Court until the conclusion of the trial or
directed otherwise.
10. The impugned judgment and order dated 17.02.2026 is
set aside.
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
         
    .....................J.
              (SANJAY KAROL)
.......................J.

NEW DELHI;

ITEM NO.23               COURT NO.7               SECTION II
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  9430/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 28-01-2026
in CRMBA No. 43979/2024 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad]
VICKKI YADAV @ VIKAS YADAV                         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH                             Respondent(s)
(IA No. 155213/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 155212/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No.164166/2025 – CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING))
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Shwetank Sailakwal, AOR
                   Mr. Mayank Suryan, Adv.
                   Ms. Abhinanda Bhuyan, Adv.
                   Mr. Alok Mishra, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Ms. Tulika Mukherjee, AOR
                   Mr. Beenu Sharma,Adv.
                   Mr. Venkat Narayan,Adv.
                
                   Mr. Akash, Adv.
                   Mr. Abijit Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Chanchal, Adv.
                   Mr. Srajan Shankar Kulshrestha, Adv.
                   Mr. Mahesh Dutt Shukla, Adv.
                   Mr. Ashish Pandey, AOR
                   Mr. Danish Alvi, Adv.                   
                   
SLP (Criminal) No.9439/2026 Page 1 of 5

--- Page 389 ---
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. The petitioner has been denied regular bail by the
High Court in connection with Case Crime No.116 of 2017
registered  with  Cantonment  Police  Station,  District
Gorakhpur, Uttar Pradesh under Sections 147, 148, 149,
120-B, 302 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short,
“the IPC”) respectively. 
3. We take notice of the fact that the petitioner is
in judicial custody as an under-trial prisoner past more
than 9 years.  The trial is in progress.  We are informed
that the trial is at the fag end.
4. The learned counsel appearing for the petitioner
brought to our notice that the co-accused of the very
same Case Crime, namely, Vaibhav Singh, came before this
Court and he was ordered to be released on bail vide
order dated 29 th April, 2026.  The order passed in the
case of the co-accused namely, Vaibhav Singh, referred to
above, reads thus:
“1. Exemption Application is allowed. 
2.  A  very  shocking  matter  with  a  very
disappointing impugned order has come up before
us early in the morning today.
3. The petitioner has been denied regular bail by
the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad in
connection  with  Case  Crime  No.116  of  2017
registered with Police Station Cantt., District
SLP (Criminal) No.9439/2026 Page 2 of 5

--- Page 390 ---
Gorakhpur, State of Uttar Pradent for the offence
punishable under Sections 2 147, 148, 149, 120-B,
and 302 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short
“IPC”) respectively.
4. Upon completion of the investigation, charge
sheet came to be filed.
5. With the committal of the case to the Court of
Sessions, the same culminated in Sessions Case
No.331 of 2017 pending as on date in the Court of
Special Judge, E.C. Act.
6. We take notice of the fact that the petitioner
was arrested in connection with the alleged crime
on 7th March, 2017.
7.  It's  been  almost  nine  years  that  the
petitioner is in judicial custody as an under-
trial prisoner.
8. What is most disappointing is what has been
observed by the High Court in Para 8.
9. Para 8 reads thus:
“The Supreme Court in case of X vs. State of
Rajasthan & Anr., 2024 INSC 909 has held that
once  the  trial  has  commenced,  it  should  be
allowed to reach to its final conclusion, which
may either result in conviction or acquittal of
the accused. The bail should not be normally
granted to the accused after the charge has been
framed. It should also not be granted by looking
into the discrepancies here or there in the
deposition.”
10. It appears that the High Court has not been
able to understand the true purport and ratio of
the decision of this Court, referred to, in para
8.  All  that  the  High  Court  ought  to  have
considered is the fact that the petitioner is
languishing in jail as an undertrial prisoner
past nine years. What more was required for the
High Court to consider the plea of the petitioner
for bail, keeping his right of speedy trial in
mind  as  enshrined  under  Article  21  of  the
Constitution.
11. We believe we should not wait even for the
State to appear. This is a gross case wherein the
fundamental right of the petitioner to have a
speedy trial as enshrined under Article 21 of the
Constitution  could  be  said  to  have  been
infringed. 
12.  In  many  of  our  Judgments  and  on  many
occasions, we have said in so many words that
howsoever grave the crime may be, but if the
accused is denied his right of speedy trial and
SLP (Criminal) No.9439/2026 Page 3 of 5

--- Page 391 ---
is languishing in jail for years together and for
no fault on his part, he cannot be kept in jail
for indefinite period.
13. In view of the aforesaid, we order that the
petitioner be released on bail forthwith, if not
required in any other case, subject to terms and
conditions that the trial court may deem fit to
impose.
14.  With  the  aforesaid,  the  Special  Leave
Petition stands disposed of.
15. Pending applications, if any, also stand
disposed of.”
5. At this stage, we need to clarify a little.  The
High Court in paragraph 8 of the impugned order has
referred to one of the judgments of this Court titled  X
v. State of Rajasthan & Anr. 2024 INSC 909.  According to
the High Court, the judgment of this Court says that if
the trial in in progress and the offence is one like
murder, then ordinarily the Court should not consider the
plea for bail and allow the trial to proceed further.
However, the dictum as laid in the said judgment should
be understood in the facts of each case.  If an accused
in in jail past 9 years, as an under-trial prisoner, then
he deserves to be released on bail because its a right as
enshrined under Article 21 of the Constitution that could
be said to be violated.
6. In  view  of  the  aforesaid,  we  order  that  the
petitioner be released on bail forthwith, if not required
in any other case, subject to terms and conditions that
the trial court may deem fit to impose.
SLP (Criminal) No.9439/2026 Page 4 of 5

--- Page 392 ---
7. With  the  aforesaid,  the  Special  Leave  Petition
stands disposed of. 
8. Pending applications also stand disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                         (POOJA SHARMA)
   AR-CUM-PS                              COURT MASTER
SLP (Criminal) No.9439/2026 Page 5 of 5

--- Page 393 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO(s).         OF 2026
(arising out of SLP (C) No(s).853/2021)
CHANDIGARH ADMINISTRATION & ANR.                   APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
NAVNEET BHARTI                                     RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellants challenge the order dated 16.12.2019
in Civil Writ Petition No.29356/2019 (O&M) passed by the
High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh.
3. The  Department  of  Education,  Chandigarh
Administration  (hereinafter  referred  to  as  ‘the
employer’)  issued  an  advertisement  on  03.11.2014,
inviting applications for filling up 489 posts of Junior
Basic Teachers (JBT) and 103 posts of Nursery Teachers on
a regular basis. Another advertisement was issued on
10.11.2014, inviting applications for filling up 548
posts of Masters/Mistresses (TGT) on a regular basis.
4. Following the completion of the selection process, a
complaint dated 29.07.2016 led to the registration of FIR
1

--- Page 394 ---
No.128 at Police Station, Sector-11, Chandigarh, in which
thirty-two  (32)  candidates,  who  were  selected  and
appointed as JBT, involved in alleged malpractices were
specifically named. Another FIR No.201 dated 11.10.2017
was registered, wherein names of seven (07) candidates of
TGT were included.
5. Taking into account several factors and attending
circumstances, the employer decided to cancel the entire
selection process, terminate the services of all those
who stood selected and appointed, and further directed a
fresh selection process to be conducted for filling up
the posts.
6. In terms of the impugned order dated 16.12.2019, the
employer’s decision to cancel the entire process and
terminate the services of all selected candidates with
respect to both the categories of the posts was quashed
and set aside. However, the Court clarified that it
refrained from passing similar orders of quashing and
setting aside the appointments with respect to those
candidates who stood specifically named in the FIR or
where  evidence  indicated  the  selection  of  similar
candidates to be tainted.
7. Aggrieved by the same, the employer preferred the
present appeal. Having heard the learned counsel for the
parties and perused the material on record, we are of the
considered view that the interests of justice would be
2

--- Page 395 ---
best  met  by  closing  the  present  appeal.  Ordered
accordingly.
8. This decision is based on the fact that the private
respondent(s) has continued to render service without any
blemish from the date of his/her initial selection and
appointment. It has been fairly stated by Ms.Aishwarya
Bhati, the learned Additional Solicitor General, that the
investigation, as it stands today, does not reveal any
role or complicity on the part of the respondent(s)-
employee in relation to the alleged crime. Further, the
investigation is ongoing, and its lack of completion is
unrelated/totally unconnected/not attributable to or with
the respondent(s).
9. However, we clarify that if, during the course of the
investigation/further investigation, any cogent material
emerges, indicating the complicity of the respondent(s)
in the alleged crime, at any point in time, the employer
would be at liberty to initiate all necessary legal
steps,  including  the  initiation  of  disciplinary
proceedings and/or cancelling the selection process qua
him/her.
10. Needless to add, the respondent(s) who is permitted
to be in continuous service shall be entitled to all
consequential service benefits in terms of, and as a
consequence thereof, the impugned order.
3

--- Page 396 ---
11. All  pending  application(s),  if  any,  also  stand
disposed of.
..................J.
(SANJAY KAROL)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI
21-04-2026
4

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO(s).         OF 2026
(arising out of SLP (C) No(s).6086/2021)
CHANDIGARH ADMINISTRATION & ANR.                   APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
VINOD KUMAR & ORS.                                 RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellants challenge the order dated 27.11.2019
in Civil Writ Petition No.26413/2019 (O&M) passed by the
High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh.
3. The  Department  of  Education,  Chandigarh
Administration  (hereinafter  referred  to  as  ‘the
employer’)  issued  an  advertisement  on  03.11.2014,
inviting applications for filling up 489 posts of Junior
Basic Teachers (JBT) and 103 posts of Nursery Teachers on
a regular basis. Another advertisement was issued on
10.11.2014, inviting applications for filling up 548
posts of Masters/Mistresses (TGT) on a regular basis.
4. Following the completion of the selection process, a
1

--- Page 414 ---
complaint dated 29.07.2016 led to the registration of FIR
No.128 at Police Station, Sector-11, Chandigarh, in which
thirty-two  (32)  candidates,  who  were  selected  and
appointed as JBT, involved in alleged malpractices were
specifically named. Another FIR No.201 dated 11.10.2017
was registered, wherein names of seven (07) candidates of
TGT were included.
5. Taking into account several factors and attending
circumstances, the employer decided to cancel the entire
selection process, terminate the services of all those
who stood selected and appointed, and further directed a
fresh selection process to be conducted for filling up
the posts.
6. In terms of the impugned order dated 27.11.2019, the
employer’s decision to cancel the entire process and
terminate the services of all selected candidates with
respect to both the categories of the posts was quashed
and set aside. However, the Court clarified that it
refrained from passing similar orders of quashing and
setting aside the appointments with respect to those
candidates who stood specifically named in the FIR or
where  evidence  indicated  the  selection  of  similar
candidates to be tainted.
7. Aggrieved by the same, the employer preferred the
present appeal. Having heard the learned counsel for the
parties and perused the material on record, we are of the
2

--- Page 415 ---
considered view that the interests of justice would be
best  met  by  closing  the  present  appeal.  Ordered
accordingly.
8. This decision is based on the fact that the private
respondent(s) has continued to render service without any
blemish from the date of his/her initial selection and
appointment. It has been fairly stated by Ms.Aishwarya
Bhati, the learned Additional Solicitor General, that the
investigation, as it stands today, does not reveal any
role or complicity on the part of the respondent(s)-
employee in relation to the alleged crime. Further, the
investigation is ongoing, and its lack of completion is
unrelated/totally unconnected/not attributable to or with
the respondent(s).
9. However, we clarify that if, during the course of the
investigation/further investigation, any cogent material
emerges, indicating the complicity of the respondent(s)
in the alleged crime, at any point in time, the employer
would be at liberty to initiate all necessary legal
steps,  including  the  initiation  of  disciplinary
proceedings and/or cancelling the selection process qua
him/her.
10. Needless to add, the respondent(s) who is permitted
to be in continuous service shall be entitled to all
consequential service benefits in terms of, and as a
consequence thereof, the impugned order.
3

--- Page 416 ---
11. All  pending  application(s),  if  any,  also  stand
disposed of.
..................J.
(SANJAY KAROL)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI
21-04-2026

ITEM NO.82               COURT NO.11               SECTION IV-B

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO(s).         OF 2026
(arising out of SLP (C) No(s).         of 2026           
@ Diary No.25854/2020)
CHANDIGARH ADMINISTRATION & ANR.                   APPELLANT(S)
                                 VERSUS
PARDEEP SINGH & ORS.                               RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Leave granted.
3. The appellants challenge the order dated 27.11.2019
in Civil Writ Petition No.20913/2019 (O&M) passed by the
High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh.
4. The  Department  of  Education,  Chandigarh
Administration  (hereinafter  referred  to  as  ‘the
employer’)  issued  an  advertisement  on  03.11.2014,
inviting applications for filling up 489 posts of Junior
Basic Teachers (JBT) and 103 posts of Nursery Teachers on
a regular basis. Another advertisement was issued on
10.11.2014, inviting applications for filling up 548
posts of Masters/Mistresses (TGT) on a regular basis.
5. Following the completion of the selection process, a
1

--- Page 439 ---
complaint dated 29.07.2016 led to the registration of FIR
No.128 at Police Station, Sector-11, Chandigarh, in which
thirty-two  (32)  candidates,  who  were  selected  and
appointed as JBT, involved in alleged malpractices were
specifically named. Another FIR No.201 dated 11.10.2017
was registered, wherein names of seven (07) candidates of
TGT were included.
6. Taking into account several factors and attending
circumstances, the employer decided to cancel the entire
selection process, terminate the services of all those
who stood selected and appointed, and further directed a
fresh selection process to be conducted for filling up
the posts.
7. In terms of the impugned order dated 27.11.2019, the
employer’s decision to cancel the entire process and
terminate the services of all selected candidates with
respect to both the categories of the posts was quashed
and set aside. However, the Court clarified that it
refrained from passing similar orders of quashing and
setting aside the appointments with respect to those
candidates who stood specifically named in the FIR or
where  evidence  indicated  the  selection  of  similar
candidates to be tainted.
8. Aggrieved by the same, the employer preferred the
present appeal. Having heard the learned counsel for the
parties and perused the material on record, we are of the
2

--- Page 440 ---
considered view that the interests of justice would be
best  met  by  closing  the  present  appeal.  Ordered
accordingly.
9. This decision is based on the fact that the private
respondent(s) has continued to render service without any
blemish from the date of his/her initial selection and
appointment. It has been fairly stated by Ms.Aishwarya
Bhati, the learned Additional Solicitor General, that the
investigation, as it stands today, does not reveal any
role or complicity on the part of the respondent(s)-
employee in relation to the alleged crime. Further, the
investigation is ongoing, and its lack of completion is
unrelated/totally unconnected/not attributable to or with
the respondent(s).
10. However, we clarify that if, during the course of the
investigation/further investigation, any cogent material
emerges, indicating the complicity of the respondent(s)
in the alleged crime, at any point in time, the employer
would be at liberty to initiate all necessary legal
steps,  including  the  initiation  of  disciplinary
proceedings and/or cancelling the selection process qua
him/her.
11. Needless to add, the respondent(s) who is permitted
to be in continuous service shall be entitled to all
consequential service benefits in terms of, and as a
consequence thereof, the impugned order.
3

--- Page 441 ---
12. All  pending  application(s),  if  any,  also  stand
disposed of.
..................J.
(SANJAY KAROL)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI
21-04-2026

(arising out of SLP (C) No(s).5316/2021)
CHANDIGARH ADMINISTRATION & ANR.                   APPELLANT(S)
                               VERSUS
SARABJIT SINGH & ORS.                              RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellants challenge the order dated 16.12.2019
in Civil Writ Petition No.29322/2019 (O&M) passed by the
High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh.
3. The  Department  of  Education,  Chandigarh
Administration  (hereinafter  referred  to  as  ‘the
employer’)  issued  an  advertisement  on  03.11.2014,
inviting applications for filling up 489 posts of Junior
Basic Teachers (JBT) and 103 posts of Nursery Teachers on
a regular basis. Another advertisement was issued on
10.11.2014, inviting applications for filling up 548
posts of Masters/Mistresses (TGT) on a regular basis.
4. Following the completion of the selection process, a
1

--- Page 464 ---
complaint dated 29.07.2016 led to the registration of FIR
No.128 at Police Station, Sector-11, Chandigarh, in which
thirty-two  (32)  candidates,  who  were  selected  and
appointed as JBT, involved in alleged malpractices were
specifically named. Another FIR No.201 dated 11.10.2017
was registered, wherein names of seven (07) candidates of
TGT were included.
5. Taking into account several factors and attending
circumstances, the employer decided to cancel the entire
selection process, terminate the services of all those
who stood selected and appointed, and further directed a
fresh selection process to be conducted for filling up
the posts.
6. In terms of the impugned order dated 16.12.2019, the
employer’s decision to cancel the entire process and
terminate the services of all selected candidates with
respect to both the categories of the posts was quashed
and set aside. However, the Court clarified that it
refrained from passing similar orders of quashing and
setting aside the appointments with respect to those
candidates who stood specifically named in the FIR or
where  evidence  indicated  the  selection  of  similar
candidates to be tainted.
7. Aggrieved by the same, the employer preferred the
present appeal. Having heard the learned counsel for the
parties and perused the material on record, we are of the
2

--- Page 465 ---
considered view that the interests of justice would be
best  met  by  closing  the  present  appeal.  Ordered
accordingly.
8. This decision is based on the fact that the private
respondent(s) has continued to render service without any
blemish from the date of his/her initial selection and
appointment. It has been fairly stated by Ms.Aishwarya
Bhati, the learned Additional Solicitor General, that the
investigation, as it stands today, does not reveal any
role or complicity on the part of the respondent(s)-
employee in relation to the alleged crime. Further, the
investigation is ongoing, and its lack of completion is
unrelated/totally unconnected/not attributable to or with
the respondent(s).
9. However, we clarify that if, during the course of the
investigation/further investigation, any cogent material
emerges, indicating the complicity of the respondent(s)
in the alleged crime, at any point in time, the employer
would be at liberty to initiate all necessary legal
steps,  including  the  initiation  of  disciplinary
proceedings and/or cancelling the selection process qua
him/her.
10. Needless to add, the respondent(s) who is permitted
to be in continuous service shall be entitled to all
consequential service benefits in terms of, and as a
consequence thereof, the impugned order.
3

--- Page 466 ---
11. All  pending  application(s),  if  any,  also  stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.            /2026
[@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.4538/2026]
R. LALROLUAHPUII                           APPELLANT(S)
                   VERSUS
   
NARCOTIC CONTROL BUREAU, 
KOLKATA ZONAL UNIT                           RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 15.12.2025 passed
by the High Court of Calcutta rejecting the regular bail prayer
of the appellant in connection with NCB Crime No.07/2024.
According to the prosecution case a consignment addressed to
the appellant was seized at the Foreign Post Office. It
contained 2.170 kg of MDMA, a contraband, commercial quantity
of which is 10 gm. The submission on behalf of the appellant is
that  the  appellant  had  not  ordered  such  a  consignment;
admittedly, the appellant was not in possession thereof and the
recovery was made from a Foreign Post Office. Besides, the
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.4538/2026                                                                               1

--- Page 479 ---
appellant has no previous criminal antecedents and being a lady
there is no possibility of her misusing the bail. It has also
been submitted that the appellant has suffered pending trial
incarceration of over two years since 07.05.2024. Moreover, in
the trial only five witnesses have been examined thus far, and
the trial would take a long time to conclude.
4. The learned counsel for the Narcotic Control Bureau (NCB)
has opposed the prayer for bail by submitting that this is a
case of an International Drug Racket. As the contraband is of a
large quantity, possibility of an accidental shipment is ruled
out. Therefore, it is not a fit case where the bail prayer
should be considered. It has also been submitted that from the
prosecution side only three witnesses remain to be examined
and, therefore, the trial would conclude in a short while from
now.
5. Having regard to the fact that there is no incriminating
recovery of the contraband from the possession of the appellant
as the contraband was recovered from a Foreign Post Office,
whether the appellant was the one who was aware of the
consignment and had ordered for the consignment is a matter of
trial. In such circumstances, considering that the appellant
has  no  previous  criminal  antecedents  and  has  suffered
incarceration for a period exceeding two years, we are of the
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.4538/2026                                                                               2

--- Page 480 ---
view that the appellant is entitled to be released on bail
pending trial.
6. The appeal is, accordingly, allowed. The impugned order
passed by the High Court rejecting the bail prayer of the
appellant stands set aside.  
7. The appellant shall be released on bail on such terms and
conditions as the trial court may deem fit to impose in the
facts and circumstances of the case. One of those conditions
would be that the appellant shall not leave the country without
permission from the Trial Court.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
………………………………………………………J.
                                        [MANOJ MISRA]
………………………………………………………J.
                                        [MANMOHAN]
New Delhi
MAY 21, 2026
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.4538/2026                                                                               3

--- Page 481 ---

Administration  (hereinafter  referred  to  as  ‘the
employer’)  issued  an  advertisement  on  03.11.2014,
inviting applications for filling up 489 posts of Junior
Basic Teachers (JBT) and 103 posts of Nursery Teachers on
a regular basis. Another advertisement was issued on
10.11.2014, inviting applications for filling up 548
posts of Masters/Mistresses (TGT) on a regular basis.
5. Following the completion of the selection process, a
complaint dated 29.07.2016 led to the registration of FIR
No.128 at Police Station, Sector-11, Chandigarh, in which
1

--- Page 489 ---
thirty-two  (32)  candidates,  who  were  selected  and
appointed as JBT, involved in alleged malpractices were
specifically named. Another FIR No.201 dated 11.10.2017
was registered, wherein names of seven (07) candidates of
TGT were included.
6. Taking into account several factors and attending
circumstances, the employer decided to cancel the entire
selection process, terminate the services of all those
who stood selected and appointed, and further directed a
fresh selection process to be conducted for filling up
the posts.
7. In terms of the impugned order dated 27.11.2019, the
employer’s decision to cancel the entire process and
terminate the services of all selected candidates with
respect to both the categories of the posts was quashed
and set aside. However, the Court clarified that it
refrained from passing similar orders of quashing and
setting aside the appointments with respect to those
candidates who stood specifically named in the FIR or
where  evidence  indicated  the  selection  of  similar
candidates to be tainted.
8. Aggrieved by the same, the employer preferred the
present appeal. Having heard the learned counsel for the
parties and perused the material on record, we are of the
considered view that the interests of justice would be
best  met  by  closing  the  present  appeal.  Ordered
2

--- Page 490 ---
accordingly.
9. This decision is based on the fact that the private
respondent(s) has continued to render service without any
blemish from the date of his/her initial selection and
appointment. It has been fairly stated by Ms.Aishwarya
Bhati, the learned Additional Solicitor General, that the
investigation, as it stands today, does not reveal any
role or complicity on the part of the respondent(s)-
employee in relation to the alleged crime. Further, the
investigation is ongoing, and its lack of completion is
unrelated/totally unconnected/not attributable to or with
the respondent(s).
10. However, we clarify that if, during the course of the
investigation/further investigation, any cogent material
emerges, indicating the complicity of the respondent(s)
in the alleged crime, at any point in time, the employer
would be at liberty to initiate all necessary legal
steps,  including  the  initiation  of  disciplinary
proceedings and/or cancelling the selection process qua
him/her.
11. Needless to add, the respondent(s) who is permitted
to be in continuous service shall be entitled to all
consequential service benefits in terms of, and as a
consequence thereof, the impugned order.
3

--- Page 491 ---
12. All  pending  application(s),  if  any,  also  stand
disposed of.

and set aside. However, the Court clarified that it
refrained from passing similar orders of quashing and
setting aside the appointments with respect to those
candidates who stood specifically named in the FIR or
where  evidence  indicated  the  selection  of  similar
candidates to be tainted.
7. Aggrieved by the same, the employer preferred the
present appeal. Having heard the learned counsel for the
parties and perused the material on record, we are of the
2

--- Page 515 ---
considered view that the interests of justice would be
best  met  by  closing  the  present  appeal.  Ordered
accordingly.
8. This decision is based on the fact that the private
respondent(s) has continued to render service without any
blemish from the date of his/her initial selection and
appointment. It has been fairly stated by Ms.Aishwarya
Bhati, the learned Additional Solicitor General, that the
investigation, as it stands today, does not reveal any
role or complicity on the part of the respondent(s)-
employee in relation to the alleged crime. Further, the
investigation is ongoing, and its lack of completion is
unrelated/totally unconnected/not attributable to or with
the respondent(s).
9. However, we clarify that if, during the course of the
investigation/further investigation, any cogent material
emerges, indicating the complicity of the respondent(s)
in the alleged crime, at any point in time, the employer
would be at liberty to initiate all necessary legal
steps,  including  the  initiation  of  disciplinary
proceedings and/or cancelling the selection process qua
him/her.
10. Needless to add, the respondent(s) who is permitted
to be in continuous service shall be entitled to all
consequential service benefits in terms of, and as a
consequence thereof, the impugned order.
3

--- Page 516 ---
11. All  pending  application(s),  if  any,  also  stand
disposed of.
..................J.
(SANJAY KAROL)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI

ITEM NO.6          COURT NO.3       SECTION II-D
S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)
No(s).  8037/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order 
dated  10-03-2026 in BA No. 549/2025 passed by the
High Court of Delhi at New Delhi]
RAVI THROUGH PAIROKAR MANOJ SHARMA   Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF NCT OF DELHI                Respondent(s)
(IA No. 134165/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF
THE IMPUGNED JUDGMENT)
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for 
hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.K. MAHESHWARI
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Anuj Chauhan, Adv.
                   Mr. Harsh Kumar, Adv.
                   Ms. Geetika Verma, Adv.
                   Mr. Pramit Saxena, AOR         
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Satya Darshi Sanjay, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Shreekant Neelappa Terdal, AOR
                   Ms. Nikita Sethi,, Adv.
                   Ms. Aastha Singh, Adv.
Ms. Aisha Thakkar, Adv.
Mr. Aditya Archiya, Adv.
Mr. Keshav Thakur, Adv.
UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the
following
1SLP (Crl) No.8037/2026

--- Page 529 ---
O R D E R
1. Against an order rejecting the regular
bail by the High Court and to seek bail,
the present special leave petition has been
filed.  The petitioner is in custody w.e.f.
11.10.2021 in connection with FIR No.273 of
2019  dated  03.12.2019  for  the  offences
punishable under Sections 406, 409, 420 and
120B  of  the  Indian  Penal  Code,  1860
registered  with  Police  Station  Economic
Offences Wing, Delhi.
2. As  per  custody  certificate,  the
petitioner  was  brought  in  custody  on
11.10.2021 and sent to the Court in FIR No.
412 of 2022 registered by Police Station
Jatwada, Jaipur.  Thereafter he has not
been brought to the custody in this case.
By the said certificate, he has undergone
three years and two months period in this
2SLP (Crl) No.8037/2026

--- Page 530 ---
case.  So far  as the  other cases  are
concerned, we cannot embark upon the facts
of those cases.  But looking to the fact of
the present case in which the charges were
framed on 04.08.2023 and by this time only
three  out  of  152  witnesses  have  been
examined,  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner contends that he has not been
produced  from  custody  to  the  Court  to
cross-examine the witnesses.  On the other
hand, learned counsel for the respondent
submits that the delay is on account of the
accused persons because they are not cross-
examining the witnesses and other accused
persons are absconding.
3. After hearing learned counsel for the
parties, we find that in a case where the
charge  against  the  petitioner  has  been
framed  on  04.08.2023  and  only  three
3SLP (Crl) No.8037/2026

--- Page 531 ---
witnesses out of 152 have been examined on
which the cross-examination is yet to be
done, endeavour ought to be taken by the
prosecution to complete the trial at the
earliest,  however,  the  delay  cannot  be
attributed to the accused. In our view, the
finding on delay recorded by the High Court
is not justified.  Hence, without going on
merits and expressing any opinion, we deem
it appropriate to release the petitioner on
bail.
4. Accordingly,  we  direct  that  the
petitioner  be  released  on  bail  on
furnishing  the  suitable  bail  bonds  and
sureties  and  on  such  other  terms  and
conditions as may be deemed fit by the
trial Court.
5. Petitioner to abide all the conditions
as imposed and shall regularly attend the
4SLP (Crl) No.8037/2026

--- Page 532 ---
trial  until  exempted  by  the  Court.
Violation, if any, may give a cause to take
recourse as permissible and the trial Court
is at liberty to do the needful.
6. Accordingly  and  in  view  of  the
foregoing,  the  special  leave  petition
stands allowed.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.
(GULSHAN KUMAR ARORA)            (NAND KISHOR)
  DEPUTY REGISTRAR           ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
5SLP (Crl) No.8037/2026

--- Page 533 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The learned Solicitor General of India, at the outset, points
out that he has come across cartoons, etc., contained in some
textbook(s) published by NCERT, which, according to him, also need
to be revisited/reviewed by the Expert Committee constituted by
Government of India, headed by Hon’ble Ms. Justice Indu Malhotra, a
former Judge of this Court. Shri Tushar Mehta, learned Solicitor
General, submits and rightly so that the said textbook shall also
3

--- Page 536 ---
be referred to an Expert Committee headed by Justice Indu Malhotra,
comprising the same members or in a different composition. An
appropriate order in this respect shall be passed by the Government
of India. Ordered accordingly.
2. We have heard Shri Shyam Divan, Shri Gopal Sankaranarayanan,
and Shri J. Sai Deepak, learned Senior Counsel, in support of the
prayers made in their respective applications, seeking modification
of paragraph 8 of our order dated 11.03.2026.
3. While we reiterate, in no uncertain terms, that the curriculum
contained in the textbook of Class 8 published by NCERT was wholly
undesirable, the Government of India has begun to take necessary
remedial steps to ensure inclusion of the true contents regarding
the Indian Judiciary by constituting an Expert Committee headed by
none other than a former Judge of this Court. 
4. However, owing to the explanation given by the applicants, we
deem it appropriate to modify paragraph 8 of the order and recall
the directions given to Government of India/State Governments/Union
Territories or other Universities and Institutions to disassociate
the three applicants from academic activities. In this regard, we
leave it to the Union of India/State Governments or the other
Authorities  to  take  an  independent  decision,  without  being
influenced by the observations made in paragraph 8 of our above-
stated order. 
5. In this vein, the opening line in paragraph 8 of the order, to
the  extent  “ and/or  they  deliberately  and  knowingly  have
misrepresented the facts in order to project a negative image of
the Indian Judiciary to students of Class 8, who are at an
4

--- Page 537 ---
impressionable age ” is recalled, in view of the decision having
been taken collectively. However, we may hasten to add that
according to learned Solicitor General, the curriculum prepared by
the applicants was not brought before the Committee at all levels,
and as such, it cannot be termed as a collective decision.
6. Be that as it may, we leave it to the discretion of the
Government of India to take an appropriate decision. 
7. Post the matter on 14.07.2026.
 
(ARJUN BISHT)                                (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)

CORAM :

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN

2
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Manoj K. Mishra, Adv.
                   Mr. Umesh Dubey, AoR
                   Ms. Madhulika, Adv.
                   Mr. Amulya Dev Mishra, Adv.
                                      
                   Mr. Shrirang B. Varma, Adv.
                   Mr. Siddharth Dharmadhikari, Adv.
                   Mr. Aaditya Aniruddha Pande, AoR                
          
                             O R D E R
The Court at the very outset record its utter displeasure at
the conduct of the respondent no.1, which is borne from our orders
recorded in the present proceedings. 
2. The  clear-cut  picture,  which  has  emerged  is  that  the
respondent no.1 to put it mildly, has tried to mislead the Court by
repeatedly coming up with proposals to clear the dues of the
petitioner  within  time  frames  fixed  and  even  the  Court
accommodating him and accepting the said request to the extent of
giving him time for six months for making the payments, not even
one commitment to the Court has been truthfully discharged. 
3. Learned counsel for the respondent no.1 tried to canvass the
issue that the petitioner has been coming up with different figures
at different points of time and the exact figure of how much the
respondent no.1 is required to pay to the petitioner, is still not
clear. 
4. In this also, we find that the lack of bonafide is writ large
on the part of the respondent no.1 inasmuch as, despite the record
showing that at least Rs.7,50,000/- (Rupees Seven Lakhs Fifty
Thousand) still remains to be paid by the respondent no.1, going
only by the principal amount, which is said to have been defalcated

--- Page 540 ---
3
by him belonging to the petitioner, today again, time is being
sought by the respondent no.1 of four weeks just to file an
affidavit  bringing  on  record  the  capacity/assets  which  the
respondent no.1 possesses. The Court would pause here to indicate
that on the one hand, there is a lis before a Court, which has to
be decided whereas, equally important is the fact that on the other
hand, a person/respondent no.1 is here, who is taking the Court
proceedings casually and has not been truthful and fair to the
Court. Thus, the Court is equally required/called upon to take a
view with regard to what has to be done so that people do not take
the Courts for granted and sanctity is maintained of the orders
which the Courts pass on the basis of trust as represented by the
parties concerned. 
5. In the present case, the respondent no.1 has been present in
Court in various proceedings and after consulting him and taking
instructions from him, learned counsel representing him had made
those commitments before this Court. The Court is not here to judge
whether the respondent no.1 is in a capacity to pay the amount or
not; fact remains that a commitment before the Court is sacrosanct
and has to be mandatorily complied with. 
6. In the aforesaid background, for the present, we find that the
respondent no.1 needs to be proceeded against strictly for such act
which is nothing short of committing contempt. Matters become
worse, as the said contempt is committed in the face of the Court
for the reason that a commitment was made personally before the
Court and the same was not complied with, not once but on several
occasions. Today, the Court has completely lost trust/faith in the

--- Page 541 ---
4
respondent  no.1’s  coming  out  truthfully  and  discharging  the
liability, which is cast upon him in law.
7. Thus, we find that for this conduct, the respondent no.1 is
required to be sent to Civil Prison with immediate effect.
8. After  the  order  was  dictated,  learned  counsel  for  the
respondent no.1, upon instructions from the respondent no.1, who is
present in the Court submitted that the matter may be listed at the
top of the Board on the reopening day of this Court i.e.,
immediately after partial court working days i.e., on 13.07.2026
and before that day, the respondent no.1 shall transfer an amount
of Rs.2,50,00,000/- (Rupees Two Crores Fifty Lakhs) in favour of
the petitioner.
9. Though, the Court is sceptical of the undertaking given by
respondent no.1, but because the matter has been vehemently pursued
by the learned counsel for the respondent no.1, who has assured the
Court that probably, this time the respondent no.1 would not fail,
acceding to the request, more as an indulgence to the learned
counsel than to the respondent no.1, the matter be listed at the
top of the Board on 13.07.2026, when an affidavit shall be filed by
the respondent no.1 before this Court bringing on record the
evidence showing transfer of the said amount in favour of the
petitioner. On the next date of listing, the respondent no.1 shall
also be personally present in the Court.
10. Till the next date of listing, the Court refrains from passing
appropriate orders on the basis of what has been recorded earlier,
relating to the conduct of the respondent no.1.

--- Page 542 ---
5
11. Learned senior counsel for the petitioner submitted that
though the payments being made by the respondent no.1 is being
deposited in a special account, but as per the Court’s order,
withdrawals are not permitted without express permission of this
Court and on the last occasion, an amount of Rs.25,00,000/- (Rupees
Twenty Five Lakhs) was allowed to be withdrawn by the petitioner,
keeping in mind the age and the health condition of the petitioner,
the Court may allow further withdrawal.
12. Having regard to the aforesaid, the Court permits withdrawal
of a further amount of Rs.25,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Five Lakhs)
from the said corpus for the use of the petitioner.
13. Before parting, learned senior counsel for the petitioner
submitted that by way of assisting the Court, a clear-cut case for
proceeding against the respondent no.1 has also been made out in
terms of the law laid down by this Court in Subrata Roy Sahara v
Union of India and Others (2014) 8 SCC 470.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

  UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                         O R D E R
SLP(Crl.) No(s).6457/2026
1. The petitioner seeks enlargement on bail in FIR
No.04/2024 lodged by the Economic Offences Wing & Anti-
Corruption Bureau, Raipur, under Sections 420, 467, 468,
471 and 120B of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 and Sections
7 & 12 of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988. The
petitioner was the Secretary, Excise Department, and it
appears that he was subsequently appointed as Excise
Commissioner, who is said to have control over the entire
process and to have been complicit in the formulation of
a beneficial policy in perpetrating the alleged crime.
The  petitioner  was  arrested  on  18.09.2025.  We  are
informed by the learned Additional Solicitor General that
the investigation qua the petitioner is complete, and the
chargesheet  against  him  has  already  been  filed.  The
investigation in respect of some co-accused is still
going on and is likely to conclude soon. The trial,
however, is yet to formally commence, and its conclusion
will  take  some  time.  The  co-accused  persons  of  the
2

--- Page 545 ---
petitioner have already been released on bail by this
Court  or  by  the  High  Court  through  various  orders,
including the order dated 28.01.2026 passed by this Court
in SLP(Crl) No.16406/2024 & other connected matters. 
2. Taking  into  consideration  all  the  attending
circumstances, however, without expressing any opinion on
merits, the prayer for bail is allowed. The petitioner is
directed to be released on bail subject to the same
conditions that have already been imposed in the case of
the co-accused.
3. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly, disposed
of. Pending applications, if any, stand closed.
SLP(Crl) No. 6562/2026
4. The petitioner seeks enlargement on bail in a case
registered  by  the  Directorate  of  Enforcement  through
ECIR/RPZO/04/2024  under  Sections  3  and  4  of  the
Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 (PMLA). The
predicate  offence  is  FIR  No.04/2024  dated  17.01.2024
registered  by  the  Anti-Corruption  Bureau  of  the
Chhattisgarh State Police for the offences under Sections
420, 467, 468, 471 and 120B of the Indian Penal Code,
1860 and Sections 7 & 12 of the Prevention of Corruption
Act, 1988
5. The petitioner was the Secretary, Excise Department
and subsequently he was posted as Excise Commissioner. He
3

--- Page 546 ---
is alleged to have control over the entire process and to
have been complicit in the formulation of a beneficial
policy  in  perpetrating  the  alleged  crime.  The  role
assigned  to  the  petitioner  is,  thus,  that  of  the
orchestrator, and he is described as one of the kingpins.
It is also alleged that the petitioner grossly abused the
public office, causing large-scale financial implications
to the State Exchequer. 
6. The petitioner has been released on bail in the
predicated  offence  by  an  order  of  even  date  passed
separately.  In  the  PMLA  case,  he  was  arrested  on
19.12.2025. There are a total of 9 accused, out of which
8 have already been released on bail in the PMLA case. It
is  not  in  dispute  that  the  investigation,  qua  the
petitioner, is complete and a statutory complaint has
already been filed against him. We find the list of
witnesses and the documents that are likely to be placed
for consideration of the Special Judge, and that the
conclusion of the trial will take a reasonable long time.
7. Keeping in view the period already spent in custody,
the fact that the co-accused have already been released
on bail and that the conclusion of the trial will take
some time, however, without expressing any opinion on the
allegations, we direct the release of the petitioner on
bail in the PMLA case also subject to the same conditions
that have already been imposed in the case of co-accused.
4

--- Page 547 ---
8. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly, disposed
of. Pending applications, if any, stand closed.

ITEM NO.6         COURT NO.7               SECTION II-E
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Criminal Appeal  No. 1854/2026
NIKHIL THAKUR                             Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH              Respondent(s)
[ONLY PRAYER FOR BAIL IS LISTED UNDER THIS ITEM] 
IA No. 132116/2026 - EARLY HEARING APPLICATION
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing
today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
For Appellant(s) : Mr. Ayur Jain, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) :Ms. Mrinal Gopal Elker, AOR
                   Mr. Sarthak Raizada, Adv.
                   Mr. Vishal Singh, Adv.
                 
Criminal Appeal  No.  1854/2026                                  1

--- Page 554 ---
 UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. This matter has come up today to consider the plea
of  the  appellant-convict  for  suspension  of  the
substantive order of sentence of 10 years, as imposed
by the High Court, and release on bail pending the
final disposal of his appeal before us.
2. We take notice of the fact that the appellant
convict was put to trial in the Court of the Sessions
Judge, Bhopal in Sessions Trial No. 267 of 2022, for
the offence of murder punishable under Section 302 of
the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short “the IPC”).
3. The first informant happens to be the sister of the
deceased.
4. It is the case of the prosecution that PW-3,
Lakshmi Rajak, is an eyewitness to the incident.  At
the time of incident, the PW-3 was in company of the
deceased.
Criminal Appeal  No.  1854/2026                                  2

--- Page 555 ---
5. PW-3 in the course of trial did not support the
prosecution and was declared as hostile. The original
first informant is not an eyewitness to the incident.
The only piece of evidence today on records seems to be
in the form of discovery of the weapon of offence under
Section 27 of the Evidence Act.
6. Although the Trial Court held the appellant-convict
guilty of the offence of murder and sentenced him to
undergo life imprisonment yet in appeal the High Court
altered the conviction from Section 302 to Section 304
Part-I of the IPC and sentenced the appellant to
undergo 10 years of rigorous imprisonment.
7. In the overall view of the matter, we are convinced
that the appellant-convict has been able to make out a
case for release on bail pending the disposal of his
criminal appeal. We order that the substantive order of
sentence of 10 years shall remain suspended and the
appellant  be  released  on  bail  forthwith,  if  not
Criminal Appeal  No.  1854/2026                                  3

--- Page 556 ---
required in any other case, subject to the terms and
conditions that the Trial Court may deem fit to impose.
(CHANDRESH)                            (POOJA SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS              COURT MASTER (NSH)
Criminal Appeal  No.  1854/2026                                  4

--- Page 557 ---
ITEM NO.3               COURT NO.15               SECTION II-B
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No(s). 1592/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  21-01-2026
in CRM-M No. 1360/2026 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana
at Chandigarh]
RAJESH @ RAJESH MALIK                              Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF HARYANA & ANR.                        Respondent(s)
IA No. 27880/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 27883/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 19-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE N.V. ANJARIA
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Ajay Jain, Adv.
                   Mr. Jinendra Jain, AOR
                   Mr. Krishna Sharma, Adv.
                   Ms. Shreya Jain, Adv.
                   Mr. M.n. Mishra, Adv.
                   Mr. Manoj Gautam, Adv.
                   Ms. Bijay Lakshmi, Adv.
                   Ms. Kashish Gupta, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Shekhar Raj Sharma, A.A.G.
                   Mr. Akshay Amritanshu, AOR
                   Ms. Nidhi Narwal, Adv.
                   Ms. Srishti Jain, Adv.
                   Mr. Sarthak Srivastava, Adv.
                   
                   Mr. Umang Shankar, AOR
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Petitioner and the second respondent’s wife entered
1

--- Page 558 ---
into a sale agreement on 18.07.2024 whereby petitioner
agreed to sell his property to the informant at Rs. 4.11
crore and he received advance amount of Rs. 60,00,000/-
(Rupees Sixty Lakh only).
2. When the deal did not materialize and the petitioner
did not return the amount to the informant, the present FIR
No. 816 of 2025, registered at Police Station Chandni Bagh,
District  Panipat,  for  the  offences  punishable  under
Sections 318(4) & 61 of Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 was
lodged in which petitioner’s anticipatory bail application
was rejected by the High Court under the impugned order.
3. On 29.01.2026, we had granted interim bail to the
petitioner.  Thereafter,  the  parties  entered  into  a
negotiations and on 09.04.2026, learned counsel for second
respondent agreed that if his wife, Ms. Manjeet Kaur, is
paid back the amount of Rs. 60,00,000/- (Rupees Sixty Lakh
only), he will withdraw the subject FIR and this court may
quash the FIR in exercise of powers under Article 142 of
the Constitution of India.
4. In course of hearing today, Mr. Neeraj Jain, learned
Senior counsel appearing on behalf of second respondent, on
instructions, would submit that the petitioner has already
paid an amount of Rs. 60,00,000/- (Rupees Sixty Lakh only)
to the wife of second respondent from an account in the
name of M/s Neha Enterprises, however, he submits that the
second respondent’s wife is satisfied with the amount
irrespective of the account from which it is paid.
2

--- Page 559 ---
5. Learned counsel for the parties would submit that
the petitioner has preferred a suit seeking decree for
forfeiture of the amount of Rs. 60,00,000/- (Rupees Sixty
Lakh only) which he has received as advance at the time of
agreement.
6. The parties would also agree that since the amount
of Rs. 60,00,000/- (Rupees Sixty Lakh only) had already
been returned, the suit No. 1832 of 2025 pending in the
court of Civil Judge, Senior Division, Panipat has been
rendered infructuous.
7. In view of the above and since the parties have
agreed, we invoke our powers under Article 142 of the
Constitution of India and quash the FIR No. 816 of 2025,
registered  at  Police  Station  Chandni  Bagh,  District
Panipat, for the offences punishable under Sections 318(4)
&  61  of  Bharatiya  Nyaya  Sanhita,  2023  and  also  the
proceedings in Civil Suit No. 1832 of 2025, pending in the
court of Civil Judge (Senior Division), Panipat. 
8. The  parties  shall  inform  the  concerned  police
station and the concerned Civil Court where the FIR and the
suit respectively are pending.
9. At this stage, learned counsel for the petitioner
submits that Haryana Shehri Vikas Pradhikaran (HSVP) may be
directed to rectify the mistake in the revenue records in
respect of the subject property.
10. Since the said aspect of the matter is not arising
between the parties who have entered into a compromise in
3

--- Page 560 ---
this Special Leave Petition, we leave it to the concerned
authority  to  take  appropriate  proceedings,  if  the
petitioner moves an application in this regard. In such
eventuality, the petitioner’s application shall be decided
on  its  own  merits  without  being  influenced  by  any
observation made in this order.
11. In view of the above, the Special Leave Petition
stands disposed of. 
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
  (RAHUL KUMAR)                             (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

1. Application for discharge of advocate-on-record is allowed.
2. Application for listing the review petition in open Court
is rejected.
3. As per the Office Report dated 10.04.2026, the  Review
Petition was found defective and the reasons thereof are
pointed out in the said report. Despite this Registry’s email
dated 11.12.2025 to the counsel for the petitioners to cure
the defects, the defects have not been cured so far.
4. Even otherwise, having perused the Review Petition, we find
that there is no error apparent on the face of the record. No
case for review under Order XLVII Rule 1 of the Supreme Court
Rules 2013 has been established.

5.   In view of the above, the Review Petition is dismissed
being defective as well as on merits.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL / CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION   
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 1810 OF 2025
NINNI SINGH   PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
HARISH KUMAR SINGH      RESPONDENT(S)
WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CRL) NO. 907 OF 2025
TRANSFER PETITION (CRL) NO. 844 OF 2025
O R D E R 
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 1810 OF 2025
1. The petitioner-wife has filed the petition under Section 25 of
the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 before this Court seeking
transfer of divorce case bearing Hindu Marriage Case No. 04 of 2025
titled as “Harish Kumar Singh vs. Smt. Ninni Singh” pending before
the Family Court, Camp Court Katghora, District Korba, Chhattisgarh
to the Family Court, Alandur, Chennai, Tamil Nadu.
2. Having heard learned counsel for the parties and taking into
consideration the grounds taken in the transfer petition, we allow
the transfer petition and direct the transfer of case bearing Hindu
Marriage Case No. 04 of 2025 titled as “Harish Kumar Singh vs. Smt.
Ninni Singh” pending before the Family Court, Camp Court Katghora,
District Korba, Chhattisgarh to the Family Court, Alandur, Chennai,
Tamil Nadu.
1

--- Page 570 ---
3. Let the record of the case be transferred without delay.
4. A copy of this order be sent to the concerned court for
compliance. 
5. If  video  conferencing  facility  is  available  with  the
transferee Court, the benefit of the same shall be extended to the
parties.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
TRANSFER PETITION (CRL) NO. 907 OF 2025 and   844 OF 2025  
7. Heard learned counsel appearing for the parties. No case for
transfer of proceedings is made out. The Transfer Petitions are
accordingly dismissed. However, the benefit of video conferencing
facility  will  be  extended  to  the  petitioner(s),  if  video
conferencing facility is available with the concerned Court unless
their presence are absolutely necessary.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Civil Appeal No.   /2026
(arising out of SLP (C) No.26807/2025)
NAVAYUGA ENGINEERING COMPANY 
LIMITED 
..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
SIKKIM URJA LIMITED (FORMERLY 
TEESTA URJA LIMITED) & ORS.
..... RESPONDENT(S)
With
Civil Appeal No.   /2026
(arising out of SLP (C) No. 27163/2025)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The issues that arise for consideration in these two appeals
are squarely covered by the decision of this Court in  Prakash
Atlanta (JV) vs. National Highways Authority of India 1.  However,
we find that out of the consortium of three companies that were
parties to the arbitral award dated 01.10.2019, only two companies,
that  is,  Navayuga  Engineering  Company  Limited  and  Sew
Infrastructure Limited, have chosen to file these appeals. The
third company, Abir Infrastructure Private Limited, did not file an
appeal. We are informed by the learned counsel appearing for Sew
Infrastructure limited that Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process
was initiated against Abir Infrastructure Private Limited under the
1      2026 SCC OnLine SC 1998.
1

--- Page 574 ---
provisions of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016. 
3. In any event, as the said company has not chosen to assail the
judgment and order dated 30.05.2025, passed by the High Court of
Sikkim in Arb. A. No.1 of 2024, no relief can be granted to it. 
4. The two appeals are accordingly allowed and the impugned
judgment dated 30.05.2025 passed by the High Court of Sikkim in
Arb. A. No.1 of 2024 is set aside, insofar as it pertains to
Navayuga  Engineering  Company  Limited  and  Sew  Infrastructure
Limited. In consequence, the arbitral award shall stand restored
insofar as these two companies are concerned.
5. The interim order dated 14.10.2025 passed by this Court in
these appeals shall stand vacated and the amount lying in the fixed
deposits shall be released to the appellants.
.....................J.
   (SANJAY KUMAR)
.....................J.
  (K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI;
May 22, 2026.
2

                             O R D E R
The matter was mentioned by the learned Attorney
General of India and a stand was taken that the order
2

--- Page 579 ---
CONMT.PET.(C) NO. 172/2026
relating to demolition has been fully complied with and
nothing more remains to be demolished. However, learned
Attorney General further submitted that he is looking into
the matter and would make all sincere endeavour to ensure
that the issue is amicably resolved in accordance with
law.
2. Learned Senior Counsel for the petitioner, at this
stage, raises an objection that the stand taken by the
learned Attorney General may not be factually correct, as
no demolition in terms of the order of this Court has
taken place.
3. Be that as it may, by way of grace, one further
opportunity is given to the concerned respondents/alleged
contemnors to take action for ensuring that either the
order is complied in full/contempt alleged is purged or to
contest the matter by filing a detailed affidavit, if
already not done.
4. The petitioners will also have the liberty to file
relevant documents by way of affidavit to demonstrate how
the order, of which contempt is alleged, still remains
unimplemented.
5. The  Court  further  clarifies  that  if  there  is  a
reciprocal obligation on the part of the petitioner to pay
rent under any agreement, on which they rely, to continue
in possession of the structure, the same should also be
3

--- Page 580 ---
CONMT.PET.(C) NO. 172/2026
discharged prior to the next date of listing to balance
the equities.
6. List on 04.08.2026 at 3.00 p.m. as part heard.
