IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (C) NO.11/2026
DUNGA MADHU LATHA                                         PETITIONER
                                         VERSUS
DUNGA RAMANA                                  RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Read the office report dated 20th May, 2026.
2. Despite service of notice of this transfer petition under Section
25 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 presented by Dunga Madhu
Latha  (hereafter  the  petitioner)  on  Dunga  Ramana  (hereafter  the
respondent), he has not entered appearance.
3. The marriage between the petitioner and the respondent was
solemnized  on  10 th January,  1999  according  to  Hindu  rites  and
customs. A girl child was born in their wedlock. However, the parties
have since drifted apart owing to differences arising by and between
them.
4. The respondent has instituted proceedings under Section 13(1)
(ia) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, bearing H.M.O.P No.35 of 2024
titled “ Dunga Ramana vs. Dunga Madhu Latha ”, which is pending
before the court of the Senior Civil Judge, T ekkali, Andhra Pradesh.
5. On the multiple grounds urged in the petition, the petitioner
1

--- Page 582 ---
has sought for transfer of the aforesaid proceedings from the court of
the Senior Civil Judge, T ekkali, Andhra Pradesh to the  Family Court,
Hyderabad, T elangana.
6. Since  the  respondent  has  not  controverted  the
statements/averments made in the petition, we are of the view that
he has no serious objection to grant the prayer for transfer.
7. We  are  also  otherwise  satisfied,  having  regard  to  the
inconvenience the petitioner is likely to face if the proceedings were
continued in the court of the Senior Civil Judge at T ekkali, Andhra
Pradesh (at a distance of around 776 kms. from her present place of
residence in Hyderabad, T elangana), that interest of justice demands
grant of the prayer for transfer. 
8. Hence, the transfer petition is allowed. H.M.O.P No.35 of 2024
titled “ Dunga Ramana vs. Dunga Madhu Latha ” pending before the
court of the Senior Civil Judge, T ekkali, Andhra Pradesh is transferred
to the Family Court, Hyderabad, T elangana. Records of H.M.O.P No.35
of 2024 be transferred to the transferee court immediately.
9. T o avoid delay, the parties are directed to appear before the
transferee court on 22 nd June, 2026. If any or both the parties fail to
appear on that day, the presiding officer of the transferee court will
fix  another  date  of  appearance.  Thereafter,  he  shall  be  free  to
proceed in accordance with law. 
10. The  presiding  officer  shall  endeavour  to  bring  about  a
settlement between the parties through the process of mediation. If
the  process  is  unsuccessful,  nothing  shall  preclude  the  presiding
officer to take the proceedings to its logical conclusion on its merits
2

--- Page 583 ---
and in accordance with law.
11. If the respondent is unable to attend the proceedings in person
on any occasion, he will be at liberty to seek permission to attend
through the virtual mode and if such a permission is sought, the
presiding officer of the court may proceed to consider it reasonably.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
………………..……………………...J.
[DIPANKAR DATTA]  
..………...……………………………..J.
[AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH]  
New Delhi;
May 21, 2026.

1. By this petition, petitioner-wife seeks transfer
of Matrimonial Case No. 911 of 2025, titled as “ Amit
Baghel  Vs  Vineeta ”  pending  before  the  Court  of
Principal Judge, Family Court, Aligarh, Uttar Pradesh
to the Court of Family Judge, Karkardooma Court, New
Delhi.
2. Though service is complete, none appears for
respondent.
3. Upon consideration of the submissions and perusal
of materials available on record, we are of the view
that this Transfer Petition deserves to be allowed.
Accordingly, Matrimonial Case No. 911 of 2025, titled
as “Amit Baghel Vs Vineeta” pending before the Court of
Principal Judge, Family Court, Aligarh, Uttar Pradesh
is hereby transferred to the Court of Family Judge,
Karkardooma  Court,  New  Delhi.  The  transferor  Court

shall transmit the entire record of the case forthwith
to the transferee Court.
4. It will be open to the parties to participate in
the  proceedings  through  video  conferencing  platform
except on occasions where their physical presence is
necessary.
5. The Transfer Petition is allowed in the above
terms.
6. Pending  application(s),  if  any  shall  stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.     of 2026
[ARISING OUT OF SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO.1342/2026]
BHARAT MANEKLAL PAREKH                        APPELLANT(S)
                           VERSUS
STATE OF WEST BENGAL & ANR.                   RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 07.01.2026,
passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Kolkata  rejecting  the
anticipatory bail prayer of the appellant in connection
with FIR No.137 of 2025 registered at P.S. Bidhannagar
(South), District Bidhannagar Police Commissionerate, North
Twenty-Four Pargana, West Bengal.
4. After considering the submission of learned counsel
for the appellant, on 27.01.2026, the following interim
order was passed:
“1. The submission of the learned counsel
for the petitioner is that the allegations
disclose a civil cause of action regarding
1

--- Page 602 ---
transfer of property, and the FIR is highly
belated.  Moreover,  no  custodial
interrogation is required. 
2. Issue notice, returnable in six weeks.
3. In the meantime, it is provided that in
the event the petitioner is arrested in
connection  with  FIR  No.  137  of  2025
registered  at  P.S.  Bidhannagar  (South)
District Bidhannagar Police Commissinerate,
North Twenty Four Pargana, West Bengal, he
shall be released on a personal bond of Rs.
25,000/- subject to an undertaking that he
shall cooperate in the investigation and
make himself available for interrogation as
and  when  required  by  the  investigating
agency. He shall also submit an undertaking
that he will not threaten the witnesses or
tamper the evidence.”
 
5. On service of notice, the respondent is represented.
6. We have heard learned counsel for the parties.
7. We do not find a good reason to discharge the interim
protection order granted earlier.  Consequently, we deem it
appropriate to dispose of the appeal by making the interim
order absolute subject to the following two conditions.
(A) The appellant shall co-operate in the
investigation  and  make  himself
available for interrogation as and when
required by the investigating agency.
(B) Within three weeks from today, he shall
submit bail bonds to the satisfaction
of the Trial Court concerned along with
2

--- Page 603 ---
an  undertaking  that  he  shall  not
threaten the witnesses or tamper the
evidence.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

                              [MANOJ MISRA]
…………………………………………………………………………J
                              [MANMOHAN]
New Delhi
May 21, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) No. 375 of 2026
RITU CHAURASIA … PETITIONER
VERSUS
NABH CHAURASIA … RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. The petitioner (wife) has filed this petition seeking
transfer of Original Civil Suit No. 225 of 2024 titled as
Nabh  Chaurasia  vs.  Smt.  Ritu  Chaurasia filed  by  the
respondent (husband) under Section 10 and 26 of the Hindu
Marriage Act, 1955 from the Family Court, Vikasnagar,
District Dehradun Uttarakhand to the court of competent and
corresponding jurisdiction at Malerkotla, Punjab.
2. Despite service of notice on the respondent, no one
has entered appearance on his behalf.  However, vide order
dated  16.04.2026,  awaiting  representation  of  the
respondent, the matter was adjourned.  Even today, there is

--- Page 606 ---
2
no one present on behalf of the respondent to oppose the
prayer as made in the transfer petition.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner and
looking  to  the  distance  between  Vikasnagar,  Dehradun,
Uttarakhand, it would be inconvenient to the petitioner
(wife) to attend the proceedings at Vikasnagar, Dehradun,
Uttarakhand.
4. Therefore,  we  deem  it  appropriate  to  allow  this
transfer petition and accordingly,  Original Civil Suit
No.225 of 2024 titled as  Nabh Chaurasia vs. Smt. Ritu
Chaurasia filed by the respondent (husband) under Section
10 and 26 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 is ordered to be
transferred from the Family Court, Vikasnagar, District
Dehradun  Uttarakhand  to  the  court  of  competent  and
corresponding jurisdiction at Malerkotla, Punjab.
5. It is on the discretion of the transferee Court to
allow the presence of the respondent (husband) virtually
until necessarily required.

--- Page 607 ---
3
6. On intimation by email, the records of the case be
transferred to the transferee Court without any delay and
immediate compliance be made. confirmed
7. The transfer petition is, accordingly, allowed.
8. Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.
…………………………………………………,J.
[J.K. MAHESHWARI]
…………………………………………………,J.
[ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]

--- Page 610 ---
1. Against an order rejecting the regular
bail by the High Court and to seek bail,
the present special leave petition has been
filed.  The petitioner is in custody w.e.f.
22.02.2023 in connection with FIR No. 24
dated  22.02.2023  for  the  offences
punishable under Sections 307, 341, 323,
337, 504, 506, 143, 147, 148 and 149 of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860 registered with
Police Station Faizpur, District Jalgaon,
Maharashtra.
2. After hearing learned counsel for the
parties at length, as alleged, the role
assigned to the petitioner is of assault to
the accused.  However, the death of the
injured was after 25-30 days from the date
of indicident.  By this time, the accused
is in custody for more than three years and
2SLP (Crl) No.8235/2026

--- Page 611 ---
five months.  The charges have not yet been
framed, however, the trial may take some
time to conclude.  Considering all these
circumstances,  without  expressing  any
opinion on the merits of the case, we deem
it appropriate to release the petitioner on
bail.
3. Accordingly,  we  direct  that  the
petitioner  be  released  on  bail  on
furnishing  the  suitable  bail  bonds  and
sureties  and  on  such  other  terms  and
conditions as may be deemed fit by the
trial Court.
4. Petitioner to abide all the conditions
as imposed and shall regularly attend the
trial  until  exempted  by  the  Court.
Violation, if any, may give a cause to take
3SLP (Crl) No.8235/2026

--- Page 612 ---
recourse as permissible and the trial Court
is at liberty to do the needful.
5. Accordingly  and  in  view  of  the
foregoing,  the  special  leave  petition
stands allowed.

stand disposed of.

 IN

  RESPONDENT NO.3
 Versus
KAVIN AND ORS.                  RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1.  The  applicant  seeks  correction  of  an  arithmetical  error  in
paragraph  14  of  the  Judgment  dated  22.08.2025  passed  in  Civil
Appeal  Nos.3132-3133  of  2023  (KAVIN  Vs.  P.  SREEMANI  DEVI  &
ORS.).
2. According to the applicant, the total amount of compensation as
enhanced by this Court is Rs.79,83,866/-. However, in paragraph 14
of  the  aforesaid  Judgment,  the  amount  stated  inadvertently  is
Rs.82,83,866/-. It is prayed that the aforesaid figure be accordingly
corrected.

--- Page 618 ---
2
3. The learned counsel appearing for the appellant/respondent No.1
does not dispute that the total amount of compensation granted is
Rs.79,83,866/-  which  has  been  indicated  in  paragraph  4  of  the
miscellaneous application.
4. In  view  of  aforesaid,  the  figure  “Rs.82,83,866”  mentioned  in
paragraph 14 of the Judgment dated 22.08.2025 shall be replaced by
the  correct  figure  which  is  “Rs.79,83,866/-.”  The  miscellaneous
applications accordingly stand allowed.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.            /2026
[@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.3761/2026]
GONIRUL MOLLAH & ORS.                  Appellant(s)
                       
VERSUS
STATE OF WEST BENGAL                  Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 20.01.2026 passed by
the High Court of Calcutta rejecting the regular bail prayer of
the appellants in connection with FIR No.0261/2025 registered
at Kaliganj Police Station, District Nadia.
4. The prosecution case against the appellants is based on
recovery of 1293 bottles of Phensedyl cough syrup from a
vehicle in which five persons including the appellants were
allegedly found.  The submission on behalf of the appellants is
that they have been falsely implicated; they had no conscious
possession of the contraband; and they are not the owner of the
vehicle. Besides, the appellants have no previous criminal
antecedents and there is no likelihood of them misusing the
bail. It is also contended that the appellants have suffered
incarceration since 23.03.2025 and the trial is nowhere near
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.3761/2026                                               1

--- Page 622 ---
conclusion.
5. The learned counsel for the State has opposed the prayer
for bail. 
6. Having regard to the nature of contraband recovered and
according to allegations there were multiple occupants in the
vehicle, without expressing any opinion on the merits of the
case, we are of the view that the appellants are entitled to be
released on bail, pending trial.
7. The appeal is, accordingly, allowed. The order passed by
the High Court rejecting the bail prayer of the appellants is
set aside.
8. The appellants shall be released on bail on such terms and
conditions as the trial court may deem fit to impose in the
facts and circumstances of the case.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
     
……………………………………………………J
                                 [MANOJ MISRA]
……………………………………………………J
                             [MANMOHAN]
New Delhi
MAY 22, 2026
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.3761/2026                                               2

--- Page 623 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Ms. Twisha Sharma, a 33-year-old corporate professional and a
former  actor,  unfortunately  died  an  unnatural  death  at  her
matrimonial home in Bhopal on 12.05.2026. A news article published
by India Today on 18.05.2026, followed by several media reports
thereafter, alleged institutional bias and questioned the fairness
and impartiality of the investigation being conducted by the State
Police. The reason attributed to the alleged lack of impartiality
was that the husband of the deceased is a practising lawyer and her
mother-in-law is a retired District Judge. 
2. It, thus, appears that the family members of the deceased
questioned the response of the police authorities and alleged undue
influence in the matter of investigation. A narrative was also
created that a fair investigation was being denied on account of
the involvement of the judiciary. It is in this backdrop that these
suo motu proceedings have been initiated by us. 
3. Meanwhile, certain developments have taken place. The Madhya
Pradesh High Court vide an order dated 22.05.2026 took notice of
one of the concerns raised by the family members of the deceased
and directed that a second postmortem examination was necessary to
clear all doubts from any quarter and to bolster the confidence of
any common person in the entire process. 
4. Shri  Tushar  Mehta,  learned  Solicitor  General  of  India,
representing the State of Madhya Pradesh, informs that in deference
to those directions, a second autopsy was conducted by a team of
2

--- Page 631 ---
doctors from All India Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi, at
Bhopal. Thereafter, the body was cremated yesterday.
5. The only issue as of now that survives for consideration is
the consent and recommendations made by the State of Madhya Pradesh
seeking to hand over the investigation to the Central Bureau of
Investigation. Since the State Government has already recommended
an investigation by CBI, the learned Solicitor General has assured
us that he will take up the matter with the authorities so as to
ensure that the CBI immediately takes over the investigation and
makes an endeavour to complete the same promptly.
6. We would like to impress upon the family members of the
victim, as well as the accused person(s), that instead of making
statements in public or before media platforms, they should get
their version recorded before the investigating agency so that no
prejudice or adverse impact is caused to the ongoing investigation.
As already observed earlier, we also request the media to avoid
recording statements of the persons who are likely to be potential
witnesses or accused, as it may unnecessarily prejudge the outcome
on certain issues, which are yet to be investigated. We will also
request the general public to refrain from speculation and have
trust and faith in one of the premier investigating agency, which,
we are sure, in due course of time will take the investigation to a
logical conclusion. 
7. While making observations above, it is clarified that we have
not expressed any opinion on the merits of the allegations or
otherwise, and it is entirely left for the investigating agency to
look into the different aspects/cross-versions of the parties.
3

--- Page 632 ---
Similarly, we have not expressed any opinion with respect to the
rights or privileges of both sides, which they will be entitled to
seek before an appropriate forum in accordance with the law.
8. With these observations and directions, the petition stands
disposed of. Pending applications, if any, also stand closed. 
 
(ARJUN BISHT)                                (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Our order dated 7.5.2026 reads thus:-
“1.Exemption Applications are allowed.
2. Having heard the learned counsel appearing for the
petitioner and having gone through the materials on record,
we are of the view that no case is made out for grant of
bail.
3. The trial shall proceed further expeditiously.
4. We are informed that the victim is missing since long.
5. We are further informed that the victim is mentally
unstable. If the victim is missing, it is the duty of the
State to trace her out at any cost.
6. We want the State to deploy special police force to find
out the whereabouts of the missing girl. Her whereabouts
must be traced within a period of 15 days from today.
7. Post this matter after 15 days to report further status
of the victim.”
2. Today, when the matter was taken up for further hearing,
the learned counsel appearing for the State brought to our
notice that in pursuance of the directions issued by this Court,
a Special Investigating Team was constituted, and the said team
has been able to trace out the missing victim who is mentally
unstable.  She has been now kept at the “ One Stop Centre,
Faridabad”.  We are informed that it is a shelter home.
3. We direct the Child Welfare Committee, Faridabad to ensure
that due care is taken of the victim in all respects.  By the
SLP(Crl.)  No.  4589/2026                                                               2

--- Page 641 ---
SLP(Crl.)  No.  4589/2026
next date of hearing, we want an exhaustive report in the form
of an affidavit at the end of the State highlighting in what
manner the victim was traced out, the place where she was
confined and who all are responsible for the trafficking of the
mentally unstable victim.
4. We would also like to have a report from the Child Welfare
Committee, Faridabad, as regards the overall health of the
victim and what further steps should now be taken to ensure that
she lives a better life.
5. List on 27.5.2026.

O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
12.09.2025 in IA(C) No. 179/2024 in RFA/36/2024 titled “ C.
Rengkhumi vs. K.Thanghmingliana” passed by the High Court of
Gauhati, Aizawl Bench at Aizawl.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the parties, we condone
the delay of 221 days, which in our considered view was
justifiably explained,  was in filing an appeal against the
judgment and order dated 18.12.2023 passed by the learned
Civil  Judge  (Senior  Division)-I,  District  Court,  Aizawl
District in Heirship Certificate Case No. 1664/2016, before
the High Court. Said appeal bearing RFA/36/2024 titled “ C.
Rengkhumi vs. K.Thanghmingliana” is restored to its original
number.

--- Page 655 ---
4. We request the High Court to decide the appeal on its own
merits, in accordance with law.

July, 2026.

7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

of this Court to a medical report of the Jail Doctor, which
indicates that the petitioner No.1 is aged about 78 years and is

--- Page 664 ---
suffering from forgetfulness, which is likely to be Alzheimer’s
disease.

prayer to release the petitioner No. 1 on interim bail, subject to
terms and conditions as may be imposed by the Trial Court.

of Rajasthan, appears and accepts notice.

     UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                                    O R D E R
1. Against an order rejecting the regular bail by
the High Court and to seek bail, the present
special  leave  petition  has  been  filed.   The
petitioner is in custody w.e.f. 23.02.2024 in
connection with FIR No.390/2014 for the offences

--- Page 670 ---
2
punishable under Sections 149, 201, 120B & 302 of
the Indian Penal Code, 1860 registered with Police
Station Barsana, District Mathura, Uttar Pradesh.
2. None  appears  on  behalf  of  the  complainant-
respondent no.2, though the matter is taken up in
the second round.
3. There were 06 accused persons and all have been
granted bail except the petitioner herein. The
present case based on a private complaint and also
on  the  circumstantial  evidence  herein  the
petitioner is in custody since more than 02 years
and 02 months.
4. Considering all the attended circumstances, the
charges have not yet been framed. At present
without expressing any opinion on the merits of
the case, we deem it appropriate to release the
petitioner from jail.
5. Accordingly,  we  direct  to  release  the
petitioner on bail on furnishing the suitable bail
bonds and sureties and on such other terms and
conditions as may be deemed fit by the trial Court
concerned.
6. Petitioner  to  abide  all  the  conditions  as
imposed and shall regularly attend the trial until

--- Page 671 ---
3
exempted by the Court.  Violation, if any, may
give a cause to take recourse as permissible and
the trial Court is at liberty to do the needful.
7. Accordingly and in view of the foregoing, the
special leave petition stands allowed.
8. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  12706/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  28-06-2024
in CRP No. 1803/2023 passed by the High Court of Karnataka at 
Bengaluru]
XXXXXXXXXX                                         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF KARNATAKA & ANR.                      Respondent(s)
IA No. 188559/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T., IA No. 188557/2025
- PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 20-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s)  Ms. Swati Ghildiyal, Adv.
                   Mr. Akshay Amritanshu, Adv.
                   Ms. Neha Singh, Adv.
                   M/s Saa Chambers, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Muhammad Ali Khan, A.A.G.
                   Mr. Sanchit Garga, AOR
                   Mr. Omar Hoda, Adv.
                   Ms. Eesha Bakshi, Adv.
                   Mr. Kamran Khan, Adv.
                   Ms. Ayesha Khan, Adv.
                   Mr. Kunal Rana, Adv.
                   Mr. Shashwat Jaiswal, Adv.
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The office report dated 19.05.2026 indicates that service
report is awaited from the Ministry of Home Affairs(for short,
‘MHA’).  We  must  point  out  that  in  this  matter  repeated
attempts have been made to serve the respondent no.2. First an
order was made on 07.10.2025, to the following effect:-

--- Page 673 ---
2
“As per office report, service is complete on respondent
No.2 through the concerned Station House Officer, but
service report has not been received so far. 
Let service be effected on respondent No.2 through the
4th Additional Principal Judge, Family Court, Bengaluru,
Karnataka. 
Additionally, liberty is granted to serve respondent No.2
through email i.e., ajitbhat1989@gmail.com. 
Adjourned to 9th December, 2025.”
2. As would be clear, this Court recorded that service is
complete on respondent no.2 through the concerned Station
House Officer but service report has not been received.
3. In the very same order, it was recorded that service be
effected through 4th Additional Principal Judge, Family Court,
Bengaluru, Karnataka. The said Family Court has sent a service
report stating that notice sent came back unserved due to the
reason ‘respondent no.2 does not live in this House’.
4. Further in the order of 07.10.2025, it was also directed
that  service  be  effected  through  email  ID
ajitbhat1989@gmail.com. The email ID is the usual email ID
used by respondent no.2 and the petitioner states that the
email was duly received and there was no bouncing back of the
mail.
5. Thereafter, an order was made on 09.12.2025 stating that
since the respondent no.2 is in the United States of America,
steps be taken for service in terms of the comprehensive
guidelines dated 04.12.2019 issued by the MHA. It is this
report from the MHA which is awaited.

--- Page 674 ---
3
6. In spite of multiple efforts made, respondent no.2 is not
appearing. While the MHA report may be awaited and as and when
it is received, an office report be put up, we by abundant
caution, are inclined to appoint an Amicus Curiae to represent
respondent no.2 before this Court, so that his interests are
also protected.
7. Prima facie  we are of the opinion that in spite of
receipt of email, no steps have been taken by respondent no.2
to enter appearance before this Court. Notwithstanding the
said fact not to prejudice him, we have decided to appoint an
Amicus and proceed with the matter.
8. We request and appoint Ms.Rohini Musa, learned AOR who is
present in Court as an Amicus Curiae to assist the Court on
behalf of respondent no.2.
9. The Registry is directed to provide the paper books of
this case to the Amicus, so appointed.
10. Let a fresh and updated office report also be put up.
11. List the matter on 25th August, 2026.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (POONAM VAID)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

--- Page 675 ---
1

FIR No.135/2025 dated 29.09.2025 registered at Police

--- Page 676 ---

anticipatory  bail  before  this  Court,  she  appeared.

anticipatory  bail  was  granted  on  22.04.2026  in

Vidhik  Sahayak  Kendra,  Bagicha,  District  Jashpur  and
taken same stand, however, we thought it appropriate that
her statement ought to be recorded by the competent Court
and it may be transmitted to this Court.
4. The supporting staff of the Vidhik Sahayak Kendra,
Bagicha,  District  Jashpur,  Chhattisgarh  was  present.
However, we called the Secretary of the Jharkhand State
Legal Services Authority(for short, ‘JHALSA’). Since, her
statement is required to be recorded by the concerned
competent Sessions Court, therefore, a communication be
given to that effect by the Secretary, JHALSA.

order be sent to the Registrar General of the High Court
of Chhattisgarh at Bilaspur, also to the District Judge

2. The petitioner has been denied regular bail by the
High Court in connection with the First Information
Report bearing C.R.No.11198067250757 of 2025 registered
at Vartej Police Station, Bhavnagar, Gujarat for the
offences  punishable  under  Sections  117(2),  118(1),
118(2), 115(2), 352, 351(3) and 54 of the Bharatiya Nyaya
Sanhita, 2023 (for short, “the BNS”) respectively and
Section 135 of the Gujarat Police Act, 1951.
3. We take notice of the fact that the petitioner is
in judicial custody since 3rd September, 2025.
4. We heard Mr. Shreeyash Uday Lalit, learned counsel
appearing for the petitioner and Ms. Apoorva Anand,
learned counsel appearing for the respondent-State of
Gujarat.
5. Having regard to the genesis of the occurrence and
other relevant circumstances on record, we are persuaded
to exercise our discretion in favour of the petitioner.
6. We order that the petitioner be released on bail
forthwith, if not required in any other case, subject to
terms and conditions that the trial court may deem fit to
impose.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.     of 2026
[ARISING OUT OF SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO.6752/2026]
SIMRANJEET KAUR                  APPELLANT 
                           VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB                                 RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 27.08.2025
passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh
rejecting the anticipatory bail prayer of the appellant in
connection with FIR No. 11/2024, registered at P.S. Beas,
District Amritsar (Rural).
4. After considering the submission of learned counsel
for the appellant, on 13.04.2026, the following interim
order was passed:
“1. Delay condoned. 
2. Issue notice, returnable in four weeks.
3. Notice on behalf of the respondent has
been accepted by Ms. Baani Khanna, AOR.
4. In the meantime, it is provided that in
the event the Petitioner is arrested in
connection with FIR No. 11/2024, registered
at P.S. Beas, District Amritsar (Rural), she
shall be released on a personal bond of Rs.
1

--- Page 694 ---
25,000/- subject to an undertaking that she
shall cooperate in the investigation and
make herself available for interrogation as
and  when  required  by  the  investigating
agency. She shall also submit an undertaking
that she will not threaten the witnesses or
tamper the evidence.”
 
5. On service of notice, the respondent is represented. 
6. Upon consideration of submissions, we do not find a
good reason to discharge the interim protection order
granted earlier.  Consequently, we deem it appropriate to
dispose of the appeal by making the interim order absolute
subject to the following two conditions.
(A) The appellant shall co-operate in the investigation
and make herself available for interrogation as and
when required by the investigating agency.
(B) Within three weeks from today, she shall submit bail
bonds to the satisfaction of the Trial Court concerned
along with an undertaking that she shall not threaten
the witnesses or tamper the evidence.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
………………………………………………………J
                                [MANOJ MISRA]
 …………………………………………………………J
                                [MANMOHAN]

RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  
No(s).  8341/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order 
dated  22-01-2026 in CRA No. 2425/2025 passed by 
the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad]
SURESH KUMAR DUBEY                   Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P.                        Respondent(s)
(FOR ADMISSION)
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This petition was called on for 
hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.K. MAHESHWARI
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Vijay Kumar Shukla, Adv.
                   Ms. Nupur Shukla, Adv.
                   Mr. Anirudh Gulati, Adv.
                   Dr. Anant Vijay Maria, AOR     
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Divyesh Pratap Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Yasharth Kant, AOR
Ms. Sonal Kushwah, Adv.
                   
UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the
following
O R D E R
1SLP (Crl) No.8341/2026

--- Page 703 ---
1. Arising out of an order dated 22.01.2026
passed by the High Court rejecting the prayer
of the petitioner for grant of suspension of
sentence, the present special leave petition
has been filed.  
2. After hearing learned counsel for the
parties  we  find  the  conviction  of  the
petitioner is only under Section 489(C) and he
is acquitted from the charge under Section
489(B) of the Indian Penal Code and out of the
total sentence of five years, two years have
already been served.   In such a situation,
without expressing any opinion on the merits of
the case and subject to final outcome of the
appeal pending before the High Court, we deem
it appropriate to suspend the substantive jail
sentence of the petitioner.
3. Accordingly,  we  direct  that  the
substantive jail sentence of the petitioner
shall remain suspended and he be released on
2SLP (Crl) No.8341/2026

--- Page 704 ---
bail on furnishing the suitable bail bonds and
sureties and on such other terms and conditions
as may be deemed fit by the trial Court subject
to final outcome of the appeal pending before
the High Court.
4. We clarify and direct that after the
release,  in  case  the  petitioner  is  found
indulged  in  any  criminal  case,  the  State
Government would be at liberty to take recourse
as  permissible  seeking  cancellation  of
suspension of sentence before the High Court.
5. Accordingly and in view of the above, the
Special Leave Petition stands allowed.  Pending
application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
(GULSHAN KUMAR ARORA)           (NAND KISHOR)
  DEPUTY REGISTRAR          ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

--- Page 714 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO. 1055/2021
I.S. INBADURAI                           Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
M. APPAVU & ORS.                                 Respondent(s)
 O R D E R
We have heard learned counsel for the appellant. We have
perused the interim order dated 16.03.2021. The said order
reads as under:
“Applications  for  early  hearing,  exemption  from
filing  O.T.  and  permission  to  file  additional
documents/facts/annexures are allowed.
We have opened the sealed cover and perused the
report. The report shows that the 203 postal ballots
have been counted, out of which 153 have been polled by
respondent No. 1 while only one has been polled by the
petitioner. 44 votes were rejected. 
If these postal ballots are counted, there is no
doubt that respondent No. 1 would stand elected while
if they are rejected the petitioner succeeds. 
The only question which has to be examined, as agreed
to by learned counsel for the parties, is arising from
the plea that the declaration in respect of these
postal ballots are not attested by a gazetted officer.
This in turn depends upon whether the Head Masters are
gazetted officers for the purpose in question. 
We endeavoured to take up the matter today but there
is no time left. We call upon the respondents to file
synopsis running into not more than two pages each on
the aforesaid issue. 
List  at  the  end  of  the  miscellaneous  Board  on
23.03.2021.”
2. The election in question was in respect of the Radhapuram
Assembly Constituency, Tamil Nadu held in the year 2016.
Thereafter, successive elections have been held in the year
2021 and presently in the year 2026 also.

--- Page 715 ---
2
3. We dispose of this Civil Appeal by keeping open the
question referred to above to be considered in any other
appropriate case.
4. The reason for keeping open the aforesaid question is the
lapse of time and the term of office having expired and no
useful purpose being served in adjudicating upon the said
question in this Civil Appeal. The High Court may now pass
appropriate consequential orders.
5. Ordered accordingly
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
…………………………………………….J.
                                           (B.V. NAGARATHNA) 
 
………………………………………………J.
                                            (UJJAL BHUYAN)    
NEW DELHI; 
May 21, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal  No(s).  2772/2026
SLP (Crl.) No(s). 6600/2026 
PEYUSH BHARDWAJ                                    Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF RAJASTHAN                                 Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 28.01.2026
passed by the High Court of Judicature for Rajasthan,
Bench at Jaipur   rejecting the regular bail prayer of
the  appellant  in  connection  with  FIR  No.  44/2025,
registered at P.S. Baggar, District Jhunjhunu.
4. At the outset, learned counsel for the appellant
submits  that  on  identical  allegations  against  the
appellant, a First Information Report (“FIR”) No. 34/2025
was registered in which the appellant was granted bail by
the order of the High Court dated 12.01.2026. Though
allegations are in respect of payment to persons not
eligible under the scheme, such payments were under joint
signatures of Sarpanch and the Secretary whereas the
appellant was Village Development Officer. 
5.  The learned Counsel for the State- respondent has
1

--- Page 721 ---
opposed the prayer for bail by submitting that FIR No.
34/2025 relates to another offence. However, he could not
dispute  that  allegations  therein  were  identical  in
nature. 
6. Having regard to the nature of offences as also the
period of incarceration pending trial and that on similar
allegations  in  another  case  the  appellant  has  been
admitted to bail, without expressing any opinion on the
merits of the case, we are of the view that the appellant
is entitled to be released on bail pending trial. 
7.  Accordingly, the appeal is allowed. The order of the
High Court rejecting the regular bail prayer of the
appellant is set aside.
8. The appellant shall be released on bail on such terms
and conditions as the trial court may deem fit to impose
in the facts and circumstances of the case.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

1

--- Page 730 ---
     UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the
following
                    O R D E R
Leave granted.
The appellant and the other accused were
acquitted  for  the  offences  punishable  under
Sections 148, 307/149 of the Indian Penal Code,
1860  and  Section  27  of  the  Arms  Act,  1959.
However, on an appeal preferred by the State, the
same was partly allowed by the High Court and the
appellant herein alone was convicted under Section
307  of  the  IPC  and  sentenced  to  rigorous
imprisonment for five years, while affirming the
acquittal qua other accused.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
We are dealing with a case of reversal.
2

--- Page 731 ---
Considering the above, we are inclined to
suspend the sentence of the appellant and grant
him bail, during the pendency of the appeal.
Accordingly, the sentence of the appellant is
suspended and he is granted bail, during the
pendency of the appeal, on terms and conditions,
subject to the satisfaction of the concerned Trial
Court.
(ASHA SUNDRIYAL)                (POONAM VAID)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR          ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

Leave granted.
The appellant stands convicted by the Trial
Court in Sessions Trial No.7-G/VII/2016, for the
offences punishable under Sections 354B, 376-D,
506 and 201 read with Section 34 of the Indian
Penal Code, 1860. He was sentenced to undergo
imprisonment for life with a fine of Rs.50,000/-
and default stipulations. However, on appeal, the
High Court modified the sentence imposed by Trial
Court to rigorous imprisonment for a period of 20
years  without  remission  along  with  fine  of
Rs.1,00,000/-
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
We have been informed that the co-accused has
been granted bail. 
In such view of the matter, we are inclined
to suspend the sentence of the appellant and grant

3

appeal.

suspended and he is granted bail, during the
pendency  of  the  appeal,  on  the  terms  and
conditions subject to the satisfaction of the
concerned Trial Court.
Tag with Criminal Appeal No.4786/2025.

no. 1 to 5 and 7. It is submitted by the learned

of the petitioner (Ajit Singh).

these matters be listed tomorrow, i.e. 26.05.2026, to
be taken up at 10:30 a.m.

UPON hearing the counsel the 
Court made the following
O R D E R
The  submissions  advanced  by  the
learned counsel for the petitioner is that
no recovery was made from the petitioner
or from the motorbike he was riding, there
are no criminal antecedents and he is in
custody since 10.08.2024.
Learned  counsel  for  the
respondent(s)  states  that  counter
affidavit in the matter has been filed
yesterday only. The same may be taken on
record. We order accordingly.
Considering  the  facts  and
circumstances of the case and the period
of  incarceration  undergone  by  the
petitioner,  i.e.  since  10.08.2024,  and
also the fact that there are no criminal
antecedents, we are inclined to grant bail
to the petitioner.
Accordingly,  in  the  facts  and
circumstances of the case, we direct that
SLP(Crl.)No(s).5735-5736/2026
2

--- Page 740 ---
the petitioner be released on bail on such
terms and conditions as may be imposed by
the Trial Court in connection with Crime
No.18 of 2024 registered at Police Station
CBN, Neemuch, District Mandsaur, Madhya
Pradesh.
It  goes  without  saying  that  the
petitioner will extend all co-operation
during the trial.
In the event the Trial Court or the
State  finds  that  the  petitioner  is
delaying the conclusion of trial, it will
be open for them to approach this Court
for recall of this order.
The  Special  Leave  Petitions  and
pending  application(s)  are  disposed  of
accordingly.

been granted bail,  we  are  inclined  to
grant bail to the petitioner.
Accordingly,  in  the  facts  and
circumstances of the case, we direct that
the petitioner be released on bail on such
terms and conditions as may be imposed by
the  Trial  Court  in  connection  with
FIR/Crime No.621 of 2024 registered at
Police  Station  Taluka  Karad,  District
SLP(Crl.)No(s).  4481/2026
2

--- Page 746 ---
Satara, Maharashtra.
It  goes  without  saying  that  the
petitioner will extend all co-operation
during the trial.
In the event the Trial Court or the
State  finds  that  the  petitioner  is
delaying the conclusion of trial, it will
be open for them to approach this Court
for recall of this order.

--- Page 757 ---
                   Mr. Rahul Krishna, AOR

                   Mr. Anmol Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Dev Vrat Anand, Adv.
Mr. Praveen Agnihotry, ADv.
 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We have heard Ms. V.Mohana, learned senior counsel
appearing for the petitioner and Dr. Menaka Guruswamy,
learned senior counsel appearing for the first respondent,
who are the parents of the minor son-Master Shravib, who is
about five years of age.
Learned senior counsel for the respondent submitted
that the respondent intends to spend some time with the
minor child during the Summer Vacation at Bhuvaneshwar;
that she would travel to Bhuvaneshwar between 26.05.2026 to
07.06.2026 (both days inclusive); that the minor child may
be in the company of the respondent between 11:00 AM to
05:00 PM on the aforesaid dates; that the respondent will
take the child from the petitioner’s house and drop him to
his  house  in  the  evening;  that  the  respondent  would
strictly comply with the directions to be issued by this
Court. Hence, the respondent’s prayer may be granted.
Learned senior counsel appearing for the petitioner
submitted that the child being an infant of only five years
of age is assisted by a maid and the maid may also be
permitted to accompany the child during the aforesaid dates
and time.
In the circumstances, we direct the petitioner as
under:

--- Page 758 ---
1) The petitioner shall enable the respondent to have the
company of the minor son on 26.05.2026 to 07.06.2026
between 11:00 AM and 05:00 PM at the respondent’s maternal
home and to any other venue.
2) The respondent shall strictly comply with the aforesaid
dates and timings and the respondent shall  pick up the
child at 11:00 AM from the petitioner’s house and drop back
the child to his house at 05:00 PM on the aforesaid dates.
3) In case of necessity, the infant’s maid may also
accompany the minor son during the aforesaid dates.
Application (IA No.148852 of 2026) stands disposed
of.
In order to explore the possibility of a settlement
between the parties, we refer this matter to the Supreme
Court Mediation Centre. 
The parties are directed to appear before the Centre
in-person on 11.07.2026 at 11:00 AM.
We request the Coordinator of the Mediation Centre to
assign this matter to Sri Rakesh Khanna, learned senior
Advocate and senior mediator.
Report of the learned Mediator be placed on record.
List thereafter.
(B. LAKSHMI MANIKYA VALLI)                      (DIVYA BABBAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that
the petitioner would be visiting India from Canada between
21.06.2026 to 31.06.2026. Therefore, on those days the
petitioner may have access to the minor child-son who is
presently about three and half years of age at Davangere,
Karnataka. He further submitted that the houses of the
petitioner and the fifth respondent are in the vicinity of
each others houses and therefore this Court may permit the
petitioner to have the company of the child. Learned
counsel appearing for the petitioner submitted that having
regard to the infancy of the child and the fact that the
petitioner did not have the chance to interact with the
child for quite some time, the mother may also be made
part of the visitation rights, subject to the petitioner
depositing his passport in M.C.No.201 of 2024 pending on
the file of the Family Court at Davangere. 
We  have  heard  learned  senior  counsel  for  the
respondent.
We take note of the submissions and we permit the
petitioner to have access to the minor child along with
his mother as well as petitioner’s parents between 11:00
AM and 04:00 PM depending upon the comfort of the minor
child and there being cordiality between the parties. 
It is needless to observe that the fifth respondent
is at liberty to accompany the minor child to the house of
2

--- Page 761 ---
the petitioner so as to enable the parents also to
interact with the minor child.

and 3(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
respectively (for short, “the BNS, 2023”).
2. We heard Ms. Roopali Lakhotia, the learned
counsel  appearing  for  the  petitioner  and  Mr.
Pranjal Sharma, the learned counsel appearing for
the State.
3. Having regard to the fact that the petitioner
is a lady and is in judicial custody past eight
months and also having regard to the fact that all
offences are Magistrate triable, we order that she
be released on bail forthwith, if not required in
SLP (Crl.) No.9607/2026 2/3

any other case, subject to terms and conditions
that the Trial Court may deem fit to impose.
4. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly,

Saharanpur, Uttar Pradesh is transferred to the Court of

For Respondent(s)  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. K.C. Jain, learned counsel for the petitioner.
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner draws attention to
paragraph 7 of the impugned order, which reads as follows:-
“7. In view of the above facts and circumstances,
since this Court has, in the above-mentioned appeals,
remitted  the  matter  to  the  Reference  Court  for
deciding afresh, which has been affirmed by the Apex
Court, as such, it appears appropriate that in this
appeal also, the matter be remitted back to the
Reference Court for deciding the matter afresh in the
light of the observations made by this Court in the
earlier order dated 25.07.2019.”

--- Page 792 ---
2
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that, if
paragraph 7 is read strictly, the benefit of the observations
made by this Court’s order dated 27.02.2026 in th e connected
matter i.e. Civil Appeal No. 3155/2026  @ SLP(C)No.26920/2019
titled as “Kalua Ram (Dead) vs. Agra Development Authority &
Ors., will not be available.
4. We are of the opinion that the apprehension is unfounded.
5. The petitioner will always be at liberty to place the
order of this Court in the above referred Civil Appeal dated
27.02.2026, before the Reference Court concerned.
6. The Reference Court concerned shall pass the order in
accordance with law.
7. With the above observations, the Special Leave Petition
is disposed of.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (MANOJ KUMAR)

For Appellant(s)   Mr. S. Niranjan Reddy, Sr. Adv. (through VC)
                   Mr. Raavi Yogesh Venkata, AOR
                   Ms. Sana Jain, Adv.
                   Mr. Kotte, Adv.
For Respondent(s) 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. S. Niranjan Reddy, learned Senior Counsel for
the appellant.
2. Learned Senior Counsel for the appellant submits that
there are multiple judgments of the National Company Law
Appellate Tribunal holding that even if the sale is on as is
where is basis, but if the sale is as a going concern in
liquidation, the entity purchasing the going concern, cannot
be mulcted with claims.
3. Learned Senior Counsel for the appellant submits that the
impugned order strikes a discordant note from those line of
judgments.
4. Liberty to place the judgments on record.

--- Page 796 ---
2
5. List on 20th July, 2026.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (MANOJ KUMAR)

unfortunately they could not reach to any amicable settlement.

30.11.2026, and shall pay the entire arrears of rent and
continue to pay the current rent.

ITEM NO.1               COURT NO.6               SECTION IV-D
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) Diary No(s). 71961/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  12-09-2025
in CWP No. 20243/2025 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana
at Chandigarh]
INDIAN BANK                                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
M/S. FAIR STYLE EMBROIDERY WORKS & ORS.            Respondent(s)
IA No. 127319/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 127322/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE
DEFECTS
IA No. 127317/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ALOK ARADHE
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Brijesh Kumar Tamber, AOR
                   Mr. Vinay Singh Bist, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned counsel for the petitioner has brought to our
notice divergent views on interpretation of Section 17 of the
Securitization  and  Reconstruction  of  Financial  Assest  and
Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002 (for short, 'the
SARFAESI Act'). The High Courts of Kerala, Calcutta and Madras
have taken a view that the Debts Recovery Tribunal (for short,
'the DRT') has no jurisdiction to condone the delay. On the
other hand the High Courts of Punjab and Haryana, Bombay and
1 of 2

--- Page 836 ---
Andhra Pradesh have taken a view that the DRT has jurisdiction
to condone the delay under Section 17 of the SARFAESI Act. 
2. Issue notice on the applications seeking condonation of
delay as well as on the Special Leave Petition, returnable on
14.08.2026.
3. The parties shall maintain the  status quo till the next
date of hearing.
(KAPIL TANDON)                                  (NIDHI WASON)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR
2 of 2

--- Page 837 ---

ITEM NO.19               COURT NO.6               SECTION XI
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) Diary No(s). 29731/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  28-04-2026
in CAC No. 6770/2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad]
NEERAJ KUMAR                                       Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
SHIV ESTATES PVT. LTD & ORS.                       Respondent(s)
IA No. 158166/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 158172/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE PETITION (SLP/TP/WP/..)
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ALOK ARADHE
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. P.N. Misra, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Abhishek Kumar Singh, AOR
                   Ms. Pallavi Baghel, Adv.
                   Ms. Anamika Yadav, Adv.
                   Mr. Kartik Arora, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Permission to file Special Leave Petition is granted.
2. The present Special Leave Petition is against the interim
order of the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad in the contempt
proceedings  whereby  after  framing  the  charges  against  the
petitioner under sections 10 and 12 of the Contempt of Courts Act,
1971, he is directed to remain personally present before the Court.
1 of 2

--- Page 846 ---
3. We are informed by Mr. P.N. Misra, learned senior counsel
appearing for the petitioner that against the main order dated
08.07.2025, the State has already filed a review application and
the same is pending consideration. He would further submit that in
SLP (C) No. 12062 of 2024, this Court while disposing of the
Special Leave Petition directed stay of the contempt proceedings
till the adjudication of the review petition is completed. 
4. Having heard the learned senior counsel and having considered
the facts and circumstances of the case, we are of the opinion that
while disposing of the instant Special Leave Petition, orders
similar to that of SLP (C) No. 12062 of 2024 can be passed.
Accordingly, we direct that the contempt proceedings be postponed
till the final adjudication of the review application is completed.
In the meanwhile, we request the High Court to take up ad dispose
of the Review Petition(s) expeditiously.  
5. We make it clear that we have not expressed any opinion on the
merits of the matter. It is for the High Court to consider and pass
appropriate orders. 
6. With these clarifications, the Special Leave Petition and the
accompanying interlocutory application(s), if any, stands disposed
of.  
(KAPIL TANDON)                                  (NIDHI WASON)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR
2 of 2

SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) Diary No(s). 25134/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  19-01-2026
in WRITC No. 1002800/2001 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad, Lucknow Bench]
RAMESH CHANDRA GAUD & ORS.                         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
U.P. COOPERATIVE TRIBUNAL & ORS.                   Respondent(s)
IA No. 139505/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 139502/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 19-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ALOK ARADHE
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. D. P. Singh Yadav, Adv.
                   Ms. Aneeta Yadav, Adv.
                   Ms. Aakanksha Tiwari, Adv.
                   Mr. Shiv Sagar Tiwari, AOR
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Against the decision of the arbitrator dated 20.09.1996, the
petitioner approached the Cooperative Tribunal, Lucknow, Uttar
Pradesh, by way of an appeal under the Uttar Pradesh Co-operative
Societies Act, 1965. This appeal was dismissed on 29.05.2001.
Against the dismissal of the appeal, the petitioner filed a Writ
Petition under Article 226 of the Constitution, which was dismissed
vide the impugned order on the ground that the writ is not
maintainable,  as  the  Co-operative  Society  is  not  a  State.
1 of 2

--- Page 862 ---
Petitioner approached the High Court against the order of the
Tribunal constituted under the Uttar Pradesh Co-operative Societies
Act, 1965. It is contended that High Court should have entertained
the petition as there is a “legal right” and in any event, as these
proceedings have statutory flavour, the High Court could have
exercised jurisdiction under Article 227 of the Constitution of
India rather than relegating the petitioner to the Civil Court.
2. Issue notice on the application seeking condonation of delay
as well as on the Special Leave Petition, returnable on 14.08.2026.
(KAPIL TANDON)                                  (NIDHI WASON)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR
2 of 2

--- Page 863 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
INHERENT JURISDICTION
REVIEW PETITION (C) NO.     OF 2026
(@ Diary No(s). 14033/2026)
IN
 
SLP(C) No. 19732/2024
VIRENDER SINGH                                Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                     Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Applications for oral hearing is rejected.
2. There is an enormous delay of 523 days in filing the Review
Petition(s).  The  application  for  condonation  of  delay  in
filing the Review Petition(s) does not give sufficient cause. 
3. Even otherwise, having perused the Review Petition, we find
that there is no error apparent on the face of the record. No
case for review under Order XLVII Rule 1 of the Supreme Court
Rules 2013 has been established.
4.   In view of the above, the Review Petition is dismissed on
the ground of delay as well as on merits.

  (PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA)

          UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Having heard the learned senior counsel appearing for the
parties at length, we find no good ground and reason to interfere
with the impugned judgment passed by the High Court.
The special leave petitions are, accordingly, dismissed.
We may note that the High Court allowed the Civil Revision
Petition and the specific prayer made in the said Civil Revision
Petition was to reject the plaint in the suit and, therefore, the
suit would stand rejected as on date, though this has not been
spelt out in so many words in the impugned judgment.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

ITEM NO.66               COURT NO.13               SECTION II-E
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No. 9336/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 07-05-2026
in MCRC No. 19100/2026 passed by the High Court of Madhya Pradesh
at Gwalior]
SHIVANI RAJAWAT                                    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH                        Respondent(s)
IA No. 153847/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 153843/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 20-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Ashish Pandey, AoR
                    Mr. Ashutosh Bhardwaj, Adv.
                    Ms. Aishwarya Sharma, Adv.
                    Mr. Shubham Saxena, Adv.
                    Mr. Prateek Rai, Adv.
                    Mr. Anmol Goyal, Adv.
                    Mr. Abdhesh Kumar, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Yashraj Singh Bundela, AoR
Mr. Surjeet Singh, Adv.
                           O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner and learned counsel
for the State who has appeared on advance notice.
2. The petitioner seeks bail in connection with FIR No.0122/2026,
dated 25.03.2026, registered with Police Station Gwalior, Madhya
Pradesh, for the offences punishable under Section 306 IPC.
3. The allegation against the petitioner and other co-accused is
1

--- Page 930 ---
that the petitioner was in an affair with the deceased and she was
in the habit of taking money and when the demand kept on increasing
coupled with the threat that she would falsely implicate the
deceased, he committed suicide.
4. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that she is a young
girl pursuing her studies. It is submitted that FIR has been
registered almost after two years and that these allegations are
blatantly false and fabricated. 
5. Learned counsel for the State submitted that the petitioner
and her sister used to extort the deceased for money which has also
been captured on CCTV. It was further contended that the evidence
which has come, indicates that even the brother of the deceased was
under pressure to arrange for money to be paid to the petitioner.
6. Having considered the matter in its entirety, we do not find a
case for anticipatory bail is made out in the particular facts and
circumstances of the present case. Accordingly, the petition stands
dismissed.
7. Be that as it may, if the petitioner appears before the Court
below and seeks bail within two weeks from today, the same shall be
considered on its own merits without being prejudiced either by the

                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Adhyayan Gupta, learned counsel for the
petitioner.
2. Delay condoned.
1

--- Page 964 ---
3. Learned  counsel  submits  that  the  case  involves
commission of offence under Section 302 read with Section
34 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short, “the IPC”).
Though the Police initially filed the closure report, on
a Protest Petition, cognizance has been taken and summons
issued to the accused.  However, the High Court has,
while considering the petition to quash the proceedings
stayed the trial.  Learned counsel submits that stay is
operating for approximately 4 years.  
4. We are of the opinion that the High Court should
take  up  the  quashing  petition  and  dispose  it  of
expeditiously, preferably within a period of 3 months
from today.
5. A  copy  of  this  order  be  communicated  to  the
Registrar General of the Allahabad High Court.
6. The  Registrar  General  of  the  High  Court  upon
receipt of the order shall place the same before Hon’ble
the Chief Justice of the Allahabad High Court.
7. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
8. Pending applications shall also stand disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)

[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  17-11-2025

SADHNA & ANR.                                      Petitioner(s)
                               VERSUS
RAKESH KUMAR JAIN                                  Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION and I.R. 
IA No. 142072/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 142077/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 11-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE N.V. ANJARIA
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Abhay Singh, AOR
                   Mrs. Ankita Agarwal, Adv.
                   Mr. Jishnu Adhikari, Adv.                       
For Respondent(s) : 
        UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Learned counsel for the petitioners submits
that  the  respondent-landlord  has  sought
petitioners’ eviction from the subject shop, which
is one amongst the 21 shops, for establishing a
super market business in the name of “Phool Chand
Super  Market”.  However,  the  respondent-landlord
has  not  established  as  to  whether  he  is  in
occupation of the remaining shops.

--- Page 966 ---
2
3. It is also argued that the present is a case
where eviction can be sought under Section-12(1)
(h) and not under Section-12(1)(f) of the M.P.
Accommodation Control Act, 1961 (for short, ‘the
Act’)  inasmuch  as,  a  landlord  cannot  wish  to
construct a bigger supermarket by evicting all the
tenants to fulfil his projected non residential
bonafide requirements.
4. It is also submitted that the prerequisites,
as  contained  under  Section  12(1)(h)  read  with
Section 12(7) of the Act have not been satisfied.
5. Issue notice, returnable in eight weeks
6. In  the  meanwhile,  the  subject  decree  for
petitioners’ eviction from the subject suit shop,
shall remain stayed till the next date of hearing.
         (NISHA KHULBEY)                    (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
         COURT MASTER(SH)                     COURT MASTER(NSH)

We  have  heard  the  learned  Senior  Counsel
appearing for the petitioner.
Learned  Senior  Counsel  submits  that  it  is
factually incorrect that the detenu had given a
voluntary statement before the High Court. In fact,

subsequent  interaction  with  the  petitioner  over
phone.

returnable on 27.05.2026.

2

detenu being taken to Australia against her will.

to accompany her.

1. The matter pertains to the appointment of Chairperson and
Members of the Delhi Electricity Regulatory Commission. Learned
counsel for the petitioner points out that the Commission has
remained defunct for the past one year as no Chairperson, who is
expected to be a former Judge of the High Court, has been
appointed. Presently, only two Technical Members are serving.

that, as per the limited instructions received so far, a proposal
to constitute the Selection Committee under Section 85 of the
Electricity Act, 2003, has been moved on 04.05.2026.  
3. It is, however, not clear as to how many days will be required
to  constitute  and  notify  this  Selection  Committee,  so  that

appropriate directions can be issued to the Selection Committee
with a view to ensuring that the selection process is completed

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  2874/2017
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated  19-08-2016 in OA
No. 263/2015 in EP No. 1/2014 passed by the High Court of
Judicature at Madras]
A. ANWHAR RAAJHAA                                  Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
S. MOHAMED JALEEL & ORS.                           Respondent(s)
 
Date : 21-05-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE B.V. NAGARATHNA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE UJJAL BHUYAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. K. K. Mani, AOR
                   Ms. T. Archana, Adv.
                   Mr. Rajeev Gupta, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Amit Sharma, AOR
                   
                   Mr. Prateek Kumar, AOR
                   Mr. Devansh Rai, Adv.    
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
This petition arises out of order dated 19.08.2016 passed
on an application seeking rejection of  the Election Petition 
in EP No.1 of 2014. By the impugned order, the said  
application was dismissed and the Election Petition was posted
before the High Court. The election in question concerns the 
Ramanathapuram Parliamentary Constituency (16 th Lok Sabha  
Elections) held in the year 2014. The respondent(s) is stated
to have completed the term and thereafter there have been 
successive elections held in the years 2019 and 2024. At  
this point of time, we find that further consideration of this
contd..

--- Page 1082 ---
- 2 -
matter is wholly academic although Election Petition No. 1  
of 2014 is still pending before the High Court.
That, in the year 2026, we find the consideration of 
this matter to be only of academic relevance and hence, we do  
not find any reason to consider this Special Leave Petition 
any further. Hence, the same stands dismissed. 
The  High  Court  may  pass  consequential  orders  in  
accordance with law.
Pending application(s), if any, stands disposed of.
(NEETU SACHDEVA)                                (KOMAL)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                                BRANCH OFFICER

ITEM NO.14               COURT NO.12               SECTION XIV
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).14319/2020
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 18-02-2020
in MACAP No.534/2017 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New
Delhi]
THE ORIENTAL INSURANCE CO. LTD.                    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
SARDAR SINGH & ORS.                                Respondent(s)
 
Date : 22-05-2026 This petition was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Pankaj Seth, Adv.
                   Ms. Manjeet Chawla, AoR
                   Ms. Jyoti, Adv.
                   Ms. Shruti Jain, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Santosh Kumar, Sr. Adv. (N/P)
                   Mr. Vibhuti Sushant Gupta, Adv. (N/P)
                   Mr. Keane Sardinha, Adv. (N/P)
                   Mr. Narender Kumar Verma, AoR (N/P)
                                      
          
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner. 
2. Though, the petitioner-Insurance Company has raised various
issues, which were recorded in the Court’s order on 07.01.2021
while issuing notice, having considered the matter in depth and
taking note of the ground reality, the factual position and most
importantly,  the  fact  that  payment  is  under  a  beneficial

--- Page 1114 ---
2
legislation and thus, the Court should not test it from a purely
technical point of view but rather, from a human angle for
determining the quantum of compensation which ultimately, the Court
grants to the family of the victim. 
2. In the present case, the deceased was 17 years old and was
survived by his parents meaning thereby, that he was the only son
of the claimants which further indicates that he was the only hope
for future of the parents, which has been snatched from them and it
is not the case of the petitioner-Insurance Company that the
claimants are in a financially strong position to absorb the loss,
an amount of Rs. 15,59,784/-(Rupees Fifteen Lakhs Fifty Nine
Thousand Seven Hundred Eighty Four) appears to be quite fair,
reasonable and equitable. Thus, without going into the given merits
of the matter, on an overall circumspection and in the peculiar
facts and circumstances of the present case, we are not inclined to
interfere in the impugned order passed by the High Court. 
3. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed. The
petitioner shall discharge its liabilities/obligation and pay the
compensation as per the impugned order passed by the High Court,
after adjusting what may have been paid earlier, latest within a
period of the two months from today.
4. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

--- Page 1115 ---
1

               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
CONMT.PET.(C) No.918/2025 in SLP(C) No.17736/2025
RAVI RAJ SABHARWAL                                 Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
PRADUMAN TIWARY & ORS.           Alleged Contemnor(s)/Respondent(s)
Date : 22-05-2026 This petition was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. S. S. Sobti, Adv.
                   Ms. Ritu Sobti, Adv.
                   Mr. Mohit D. Ram, AoR
                   Ms. Nayan Gupta, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Praveen Kumar Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Gunnam Venkateswara Rao, AoR
                   Mr. C. Sanal Nambiar, Adv.
                   Ms. Abhinav Shailly, Adv.
                   Ms. Chetna Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Swati Dwivedi, Adv.
                   Ms. Devyani Mahra, Adv.
                   Mr. Ritesh Kumar, Adv.                   
                   
          
                             O R D E R
At the outset, learned counsel for the petitioner submits that
there were two portions of the order, which were to be complied
with. One with regard to vacation of premises and other with regard
to paying the due rent. It was submitted that the premises in
question was vacated but after forty-five days from the time

--- Page 1120 ---
2
indicated in the order dated 18.07.2025 passed by this Court and
more importantly, the rent which admittedly was Rs.1,65,000/-
(Rupees One Lakh Sixty Five Thousand) per month but the respondent
has paid only 75 per cent of the same. 
2. In  support  of  his  contention,  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner submits that the last agreement between the parties was
for eleven months in which, the monthly rent was Rs.1,65,000/-
(Rupees One Lakh Sixty Five Thousand) per month and thereafter, as
mutually agreed, the respondents have continued as a tenant on a
month-to-month payment basis.
3. Learned counsel for the respondents disputes the said claim.
4. In view of the aforesaid, let the respondents file a detailed
computation chart with regard to how much they have paid to the
petitioner towards rent of the premises in question.
5. At the same time, it shall be open for the petitioner to bring
on record, if already not on record, the documents to show that the
liability to pay rent was Rs.1,65,000/- (Rupees One Lakh Sixty Five
Thousand) per month.
6. List on 20.07.2026.
7. The  personal  presence  of  the  alleged  contemnors  stands
dispensed with, for the present.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

Leave granted.
The appellant stands convicted by the Trial
1

--- Page 1130 ---
Court in the Sessions Trial No.40 of 2012 for the
offences  punishable  under  Section  302  of  the
Indian Penal Code, 1860. He has been sentenced to
undergo rigorous imprisonment for life along with
payment  of  fine  of  Rs.10,000/-  with  default
stipulations.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
Taking  into  consideration  the  period  of
incarceration undergone by the appellant, we are
inclined to suspend the sentence of the appellant
and grant him bail, during the pendency of the
present appeal.
Accordingly, the sentence of the appellant is
suspended and he is granted bail, during the
pendency  of  the  appeal,  on  the  terms  and
conditions subject to the satisfaction of the
concerned Trial Court.

ITEM NO.77                   COURT NO.8                  SECTION XV
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (C) No.18865/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 18-05-2026 
in DBSAW No. 359/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature for 
Rajasthan at Jaipur]
SHREE RAM COLONY BVIKAS SAMITI                        Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF RAJASTHAN & ORS.                             Respondent(s)
[FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 164432/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 164434/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES]
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE DIPANKAR DATTA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Arunabh Choudhary, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Megha Karnwal, AOR
                   Mr. Ashish Sharma, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Anuj Bhandari, AOR
                   Ms. Ishu Bhardwaj, Adv.
                   Ms. Jahanvi Bhardwaj, Adv.
                                      
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Prima  facie,  we  are  satisfied  that  the  High  Court  was  not
justified in vacating the order of stay granted earlier on April 21, 2026
while  hearing  an  application  for  impleadment.  The  immediate
consequence  has  been  that  the  respondents  have  commenced
demolition of alleged unauthorized structures.
2. Be that as it may, we are informed that around 10 structures
still remain to be demolished.
1

--- Page 1194 ---
3. We  restrain  the  respondents  from  demolishing  any  further
structure.
4. At this stage, the parties agree to disposal of the special leave
petition at the admission stage. 
5. The  special  leave  petition  stands  disposed  of  with  the
observation that the special appeal, pending before the High Court,
will be decided in accordance with law.
6. The  order  passed  by us  restraining  further  demolition  shall
continue till the special appeal is disposed of. Should an application
for  vacating  this  restraining  order  be  filed,  the  High  Court  may
proceed to decide the same upon hearing the parties.
7. All points and contentions on merits are left open.
8. Learned  counsel  for  the  respondents  shall  immediately
communicate this order for securing compliance.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
(KRITIKA TIWARI)                             (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                       COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

--- Page 1195 ---

in SBCRLMBT(SOS)A No. 692/2024 passed by the High Court of 
Judicature for Rajasthan at Jaipur]
AAKIL                                              Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF RAJASTHAN                                 Respondent(s)
(IA No. 66103/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT IA No. 66104/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PANKAJ MITHAL
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE S.V.N. BHATTI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Vinay Kumar Aherodiya, Adv.
                   Ms. Sonu Kumari, Adv.
                   Mr. Jugul Kishor Gupta, AOR
                   Mr. Vijay Pal, Adv.
                   Mr. Ankit Borker, Adv.
                   Mr. Alok Singh, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Padmesh Mishra, A.A.G.
                   Mr. S. Udaya Kumar Sagar, AOR
                   
                   
    UPON hearing the counsel the court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. The  petitioner  has  been  denied  suspension  of
sentence pending criminal appeal against his conviction
for an offence punishable under Sections 376, 506 IPC and
Sections 3/4 of the POCSO Act and sentence of 20 years of
1

--- Page 1212 ---
rigorous imprisonment.
3. The petitioner has only under gone 5 years of
sentence. The conviction is based upon the DNA Report.
4. Thus, in the facts and circumstances of the case,
we do not consider it to be a fit case for grant of
suspension of sentence at this stage.
5. The petition is, accordingly, dismissed. However,
the petitioner is at liberty to renew the prayer for
suspension of sentence after 2 years.
6. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.
   (Nidhi Mathur)  (Geeta Ahuja)
Court Master (NSH) Deputy Registrar

to withdraw the Special Leave Petition.

For Respondent(s)  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
The application for exemption from filing the
official translation is allowed.
Learned counsel appearing for the petitioner
submitted  that  it  is  not  possible  to  seek  a
reference before the jurisdictional Court which has
got a limited role, that which is to decide the
reference alone. In the case at hand, the Award is
in favour of the petitioner who is aggrieved by the
decision of the Sub-Divisional Officer.
Issue notice, returnable on 10.08.2026.

(IRAMNAZ)                                       (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
